facts.fact: Texas was an independent nation from 1836 to 1845.
facts.background: Texas operated as the Republic of Texas for nearly ten years before joining the United States.

facts.fact: The Battle of San Jacinto lasted only 18 minutes but secured Texas independence.
facts.background: Sam Houston’s army defeated Santa Anna, marking the decisive victory of the Texas Revolution.

facts.fact: The Alamo fell after a 13-day siege during the Texas Revolution.
facts.background: Mexican forces under Santa Anna overran the Texian defenders at the Alamo in San Antonio. The battle became a rallying cry for independence with the phrase "Remember the Alamo."

facts.fact: Texas joined the United States as the 28th state.
facts.background: After nearly a decade as an independent republic, Texas was admitted to the Union as the 28th state, a decision that helped set the stage for the Mexican–American War.

facts.fact: Juneteenth originated in Galveston, Texas.
facts.background: Union General Gordon Granger arrived in Galveston and announced General Order No. 3, informing enslaved people in Texas of their freedom more than two years after the Emancipation Proclamation.

facts.fact: The Great Galveston Hurricane of 1900 is the deadliest natural disaster in U.S. history.
facts.background: A powerful hurricane destroyed much of Galveston Island and killed thousands of residents, reshaping the region and contributing to Houston’s rise as a major port city.

facts.fact: The Spindletop gusher near Beaumont launched the Texas oil boom.
facts.background: When the Spindletop well blew, it produced an unprecedented flow of oil, attracting investment and helping create major oil companies that shaped the 20th-century energy industry.

facts.fact: Bluebonnets were named the official state flower of Texas in 1901.
facts.background: The Texas Legislature chose the bluebonnet as the state flower, and every spring fields of these blue blossoms cover roadsides and ranchlands across central Texas.

facts.fact: Dr Pepper was invented in Waco, Texas, in the 1880s.
facts.background: Pharmacist Charles Alderton created the flavored soft drink at a Waco drugstore, making Dr Pepper one of the oldest major soda brands in the United States.

facts.fact: Chili con carne is the official state dish of Texas.
facts.background: In 1977, the Texas Legislature declared chili the state dish. Many Texans insist that traditional Texas chili contains meat and spices—but no beans.

facts.fact: Six different flags have flown over Texas.
facts.background: The six flags represent the nations that have claimed sovereignty over Texas: Spain, France, Mexico, the Republic of Texas, the Confederate States of America, and the United States.

facts.fact: Houston is home to NASA’s Johnson Space Center and Mission Control.
facts.background: NASA located its Manned Spacecraft Center, later renamed Johnson Space Center, in Houston, where Mission Control has overseen Gemini, Apollo, Space Shuttle, and International Space Station missions.

facts.fact: Guadalupe Peak is the highest point in Texas at 8,751 feet.
facts.background: Located in the Guadalupe Mountains of far West Texas, Guadalupe Peak offers sweeping views of desert and mountains and is a popular destination for hikers.

facts.fact: Big Bend National Park protects a dramatic bend of the Rio Grande along the U.S.–Mexico border.
facts.background: Established as a national park in 1944, Big Bend preserves desert landscapes, the Chisos Mountains, and deep canyons carved by the Rio Grande.

facts.fact: Texas has 254 counties, more than any other U.S. state.
facts.background: The high number of counties dates back to the era of travel by horseback, when local governments needed to be within a day’s ride for most residents.

facts.fact: Caddo Lake, on the Texas–Louisiana border, is one of the few naturally formed large lakes in Texas.
facts.background: Caddo Lake is known for its maze of bayous, cypress trees, and rich wildlife, contrasting with the many man-made reservoirs found elsewhere in Texas.

facts.fact: The official nickname of Texas is "The Lone Star State."
facts.background: The lone star symbolizes Texas’s history as an independent republic and its enduring spirit of independence and unity.

facts.fact: Texas has no state income tax.
facts.background: Instead of taxing personal income, Texas raises revenue primarily through sales taxes, property taxes, and various fees.

facts.fact: The Texas Legislature meets in regular session only once every two years.
facts.background: Regular legislative sessions occur in odd-numbered years and can last up to 140 days, reflecting Texas’s tradition of a part-time citizen legislature.

facts.fact: The San Antonio River Walk is one of the most visited attractions in Texas.
facts.background: Built along the San Antonio River below street level, the River Walk features shops, restaurants, historic sites, and boat tours that draw millions of visitors each year.

facts.fact: The Texas Rangers are one of the oldest statewide law enforcement agencies in the United States.
facts.background: Founded by Stephen F. Austin, the Rangers initially served as a volunteer militia and later became a formal law enforcement agency known for frontier defense, criminal investigations, and major casework.

facts.fact: Texas has more cattle than any other state in the U.S.
facts.background: The state's cattle population often exceeds 12 million, supporting ranching traditions, beef production, and major livestock auctions across rural regions.

facts.fact: Austin is known as the 'Live Music Capital of the World.'
facts.background: The city earned its nickname due to its high density of live music venues and festivals such as Austin City Limits and South by Southwest.

facts.fact: The Rio Grande serves as the natural border between Texas and Mexico for over 1,200 miles.
facts.background: The river flows from Colorado through New Mexico and along the Texas–Mexico border, shaping ecosystems, agriculture, and international boundaries.

facts.fact: Sam Houston is the only person to serve as president of a republic, governor of two different U.S. states, and a U.S. senator.
facts.background: Houston served as president of the Republic of Texas, governor of Texas and Tennessee, and later represented Texas in the U.S. Senate.

facts.fact: Texas is the second-largest U.S. state in both area and population.
facts.background: Only Alaska is larger in land area, and only California has a larger population. Texas’s size influences its diverse landscapes and regional cultures.

facts.fact: The Houston Astrodome was the world's first domed sports stadium.
facts.background: Nicknamed the "Eighth Wonder of the World," the Astrodome introduced innovations such as artificial turf and climate-controlled sporting environments.

facts.fact: Texas once tried to divide itself into multiple states after joining the Union.
facts.background: The annexation resolution allowed Texas to split into as many as five states, though it never exercised this option despite periodic political discussions.

facts.fact: The Palo Duro Canyon is the second-largest canyon in the United States.
facts.background: Located in the Texas Panhandle, Palo Duro Canyon stretches roughly 120 miles long and 20 miles wide, earning the nickname 'The Grand Canyon of Texas.'

facts.fact: Barbara Jordan was the first African American woman elected to the Texas Senate and later to the U.S. House of Representatives from the South.
facts.background: Jordan was known for her powerful oratory and leadership in civil rights, constitutional matters, and ethics in government.

facts.fact: Spanish missions played a major role in early Texas settlement.
facts.background: Missions such as San Antonio de Valero (the Alamo) were established to convert Indigenous peoples and secure Spanish presence in the region.

facts.fact: Texas is home to more than 6,000 plant species, one of the highest totals of any U.S. state.
facts.background: Its diverse climate regions—from Piney Woods to desert mountains—support a wide range of plant life, including numerous endemic species.

facts.fact: The State Fair of Texas in Dallas is one of the largest and longest-running fairs in the nation.
facts.background: Famous for its fried food, livestock shows, carnival rides, and the iconic Big Tex statue, the fair draws millions of visitors annually.

facts.fact: Lyndon B. Johnson, the 36th President of the United States, was born in Stonewall, Texas.
facts.background: LBJ played key roles in civil rights legislation, Medicare, and the space race, and his ranch near Johnson City is now a national historical park.

facts.fact: The Texas coastline stretches over 350 miles along the Gulf of Mexico.
facts.background: Beaches, marshes, barrier islands, and ports along the Texas Gulf Coast support tourism, fishing, and the state's energy industry.

facts.fact: Texas is the nation’s leading producer of wind energy.
facts.background: Large-scale wind farms in West Texas and the Panhandle generate more renewable electricity than any other U.S. state.

facts.fact: Selena Quintanilla-Pérez, known as the 'Queen of Tejano Music,' was born in Lake Jackson, Texas.
facts.background: Selena became an icon of Latin music and culture, influencing future generations of artists and receiving numerous posthumous honors.

facts.fact: Texas has the largest number of public school students in the United States after California.
facts.background: The Texas Education Agency oversees more than 1,200 school districts, serving over 5 million students across the state.

facts.fact: The Hill Country is famous for its limestone cliffs, spring-fed rivers, and wildflower blooms.
facts.background: Stretching through central Texas, the Hill Country attracts visitors for swimming holes, scenic drives, wineries, and ranchland views.

facts.fact: Tex-Mex cuisine originated along the Texas–Mexico border.
facts.background: Combining Mexican dishes with Texas ingredients, Tex-Mex includes staples such as fajitas, chili con carne, enchiladas, and queso.

facts.fact: The Texas State Capitol in Austin is taller than the U.S. Capitol in Washington, D.C.
facts.background: Completed in 1888, the Texas State Capitol stands about 308 feet tall, intentionally built slightly higher than the U.S. Capitol as a point of pride.

facts.fact: The King Ranch in South Texas is larger than the entire state of Rhode Island.
facts.background: Covering more than 800,000 acres, King Ranch is one of the largest and most famous ranches in the world, symbolizing the scale of Texas ranching.

facts.fact: Texas law allows the state flag to fly at the same height as the U.S. flag on a separate pole, reflecting its history as an independent republic.
facts.background: Texas state law specifies that the Lone Star Flag may fly at equal height to the U.S. flag on separate poles, a tradition tied to Texas’s unique history as an independent republic from 1836 to 1845.

facts.fact: The phrase "Don’t Mess with Texas" began as an anti-litter campaign slogan.
facts.background: Launched in the 1980s, the campaign used bold messaging and celebrity endorsements to reduce roadside litter and became part of Texas pop culture.
facts.source: Texas Department of Transportation campaign history

facts.fact: San Antonio is home to one of the largest concentrations of Spanish colonial missions in North America.
facts.background: The San Antonio Missions, including the Alamo, are preserved as a UNESCO World Heritage Site recognizing their historical and cultural significance.

facts.fact: The Chihuahuan Desert, the largest desert in North America, extends deep into West Texas.
facts.background: West Texas sits within the Chihuahuan Desert, which spans parts of Texas, New Mexico, Arizona, and northern Mexico. The region's rugged desert landscapes shape the climate, wildlife, and culture of the Trans-Pecos area.

facts.fact: Texas leads the nation in cotton production.
facts.background: Cotton has been a cornerstone of Texas agriculture for more than a century, with large acreages in the High Plains and other regions.

facts.fact: The official state tree of Texas is the pecan tree.
facts.background: The Texas Legislature designated the pecan as the state tree, reflecting its importance as a native species and a key ingredient in Texas desserts like pecan pie.

facts.fact: The official state bird of Texas is the mockingbird.
facts.background: Mockingbirds are known for their ability to mimic the songs of other birds and even man-made sounds, and are common in urban and rural areas across Texas.

facts.fact: Texas barbecue is world-famous for its emphasis on smoked beef brisket.
facts.background: Central Texas barbecue traditions focus on slow-smoked beef, often seasoned simply with salt and pepper and cooked over oak or mesquite.

facts.fact: The Dallas–Fort Worth Metroplex is one of the largest inland metropolitan areas in the United States.
facts.background: The region includes dozens of cities and suburbs, serving as a major center for finance, technology, transportation, and sports.

facts.fact: Texas was the birthplace of modern rodeo.
facts.background: Rodeo grew out of cattle handling and ranch skills in Texas and the Southwest, evolving into organized competitions featuring bull riding, roping, and barrel racing.

facts.fact: Lady Bird Johnson, from Texas, championed highway beautification across the United States.
facts.background: As First Lady, Claudia "Lady Bird" Johnson promoted wildflower planting and scenic preservation, influencing the look of highways in Texas and beyond.

facts.fact: The Texas Hill Country is known for its wildflowers, especially bluebonnets and Indian paintbrush.
facts.background: Each spring, highways and backroads in central Texas are lined with colorful wildflowers, drawing photographers and travelers from across the state.

facts.fact: The Port of Houston is one of the busiest ports in the United States by tonnage.
facts.background: Located along the Houston Ship Channel, the port handles large volumes of petroleum, chemicals, and container cargo, supporting global trade.

facts.fact: The Texas Longhorn is a symbol of the state’s ranching heritage.
facts.background: Longhorn cattle are known for their distinctive long horns and hardiness, playing a key role in cattle drives and early Texas ranching.

facts.fact: One of the most famous transmissions in history — 'Houston, Tranquility Base here. The Eagle has landed' — was spoken from the Moon to NASA's Mission Control in Houston.
facts.background: During the Apollo 11 lunar landing on July 20, 1969, Neil Armstrong radioed Mission Control in Houston with the iconic words confirming humanity's first successful Moon landing.

facts.fact: The Panhandle-Plains region of Texas experiences some of the most dramatic weather in the state.
facts.background: The High Plains can see blizzards, severe thunderstorms, hail, and dust storms, reflecting its location where different air masses collide.

facts.fact: Texas A&M University in College Station began as a land-grant agricultural and mechanical college.
facts.background: Founded in 1876, Texas A&M grew from a small military and agricultural school into a major research university with a strong Corps of Cadets tradition.

facts.fact: The University of Texas at Austin is one of the largest public universities in the United States.
facts.background: Established in 1883, UT Austin is known for its research programs, tower landmark, and role in Texas politics, technology, and culture.

facts.fact: Houston’s population makes it the largest city in Texas and one of the most diverse cities in the nation.
facts.background: Residents of Houston trace their roots to countries all over the world, contributing to a rich blend of languages, cuisines, and cultural festivals.

facts.fact: El Paso is closer to San Diego, California, than it is to Houston, Texas.
facts.background: Texas’s vast size means that some cities within the state are geographically farther apart than they are from cities in other states.

facts.fact: The Texas State Fair’s icon, Big Tex, is a 55-foot-tall cowboy figure.
facts.background: Big Tex has greeted visitors to the State Fair of Texas for decades, becoming a recognizable symbol of Dallas and Texas fair culture.

facts.fact: The Texas Horned Lizard, sometimes called the 'horny toad,' is the state reptile.
facts.background: This small, spiky lizard can squirt blood from its eyes as a defense mechanism and has seen population declines in some parts of Texas.

facts.fact: Austin’s Barton Springs Pool is a famous spring-fed swimming pool in the heart of the city.
facts.background: Fed by underground springs, Barton Springs maintains cool temperatures year-round and is a popular gathering place for locals and visitors.

facts.fact: The Texas State Railroad offers historic train rides through the Piney Woods of East Texas.
facts.background: Originally built to serve a state prison foundry, the line now provides scenic excursions using vintage locomotives and cars.

facts.fact: Texas has multiple professional sports teams in the NFL, NBA, MLB, NHL, and MLS.
facts.background: Teams such as the Dallas Cowboys, Houston Texans, San Antonio Spurs, Houston Rockets, Dallas Mavericks, Texas Rangers, Houston Astros, Dallas Stars, and various soccer clubs reflect the state’s sports enthusiasm.

facts.fact: The Texas Medical Center in Houston is the largest medical complex in the world.
facts.background: The complex includes dozens of hospitals, research institutions, and specialty centers, attracting patients and professionals from around the globe.

facts.fact: The official state small mammal of Texas is the nine-banded armadillo.
facts.background: Armadillos are common across much of Texas and are known for their armored shells and digging habits.

facts.fact: Texas’s first major oil discovery at Spindletop helped shift the world from coal to oil as a primary energy source.
facts.background: The enormous flow of oil at Spindletop led to rapid industrial growth, new refineries, and the rise of modern petroleum companies.

facts.fact: The city of San Marcos is known for having one of the largest aquifer-fed spring systems in the state.
facts.background: Spring-fed rivers such as the San Marcos and Comal Rivers provide clear, cool water for recreation and habitat for endangered species.

facts.fact: The Texas chili cook-off tradition includes famous events like the Terlingua Chili Cookoff.
facts.background: Chili competitions draw cooks and tasters from across the state and beyond, often featuring strong opinions about ingredients and spice levels.

facts.fact: The Texas Constitution has been amended hundreds of times since its adoption in 1876.
facts.background: The document is long and detailed, requiring frequent amendments to address policy changes and new state programs.

facts.fact: The Enchanted Rock near Fredericksburg is a massive pink granite dome rising above the Hill Country.
facts.background: Enchanted Rock State Natural Area preserves this geological formation, which has long been the subject of Native American legends and modern hiking trips.

facts.fact: Texas music styles include country, blues, Tejano, conjunto, and outlaw country.
facts.background: Artists from Willie Nelson and Beyoncé to Selena and Stevie Ray Vaughan reflect the range of musical traditions developed or nurtured in Texas.

facts.fact: Marfa, Texas, is known for mysterious 'Marfa Lights' reported near the town.
facts.background: Unexplained glowing lights have been seen in the desert near Marfa for more than a century, inspiring folklore, studies, and tourism.

facts.fact: The Texas High Plains produce significant amounts of grain, cotton, and cattle.
facts.background: Irrigated agriculture and large ranches in the Panhandle contribute to both state and national food supplies.

facts.fact: The Battle of Gonzales is remembered for the "Come and Take It" flag.
facts.background: Texian settlers refused to surrender a small cannon to Mexican forces, raising a flag with a black star and the phrase "Come and Take It," symbolizing resistance.

facts.fact: Texas has multiple national parks and seashores, including Big Bend and Padre Island National Seashore.
facts.background: These federally protected areas preserve diverse landscapes from desert mountains to barrier island beaches.

facts.fact: The city of Waco is home to the Dr Pepper Museum, celebrating the soft drink’s origins.
facts.background: The museum explores the history of Dr Pepper and soft-drink bottling, highlighting Waco’s role in beverage history.

facts.fact: Texas has one of the largest networks of state parks in the United States.
facts.background: From Piney Woods forests to desert canyons and Gulf Coast beaches, Texas state parks protect natural areas and provide camping, hiking, and fishing opportunities.

facts.fact: The Texas State Library and Archives Commission preserves key documents of Texas history.
facts.background: The archives hold early maps, government records, and personal papers that tell the story of Texas from Spanish rule to the present.

facts.fact: The Balcones Escarpment marks a major geological fault line across central Texas.
facts.background: This natural boundary influences river courses, springs, and the transition from the Hill Country to the coastal plains.

facts.fact: The Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo is one of the largest livestock and rodeo events in the world.
facts.background: The event combines livestock competitions, rodeo performances, concerts, and educational programs, drawing millions of visitors each year.

facts.fact: The official state motto of Texas is 'Friendship.'
facts.background: The motto reflects the origin of the word 'Texas,' which comes from a Caddo word often translated as 'friends' or 'allies.'

facts.fact: Texas shares a border with four U.S. states and one country.
facts.background: Texas borders New Mexico, Oklahoma, Arkansas, and Louisiana, as well as the country of Mexico along the Rio Grande.

facts.fact: Space Center Houston serves as the official visitor center for NASA’s Johnson Space Center.
facts.background: The center offers exhibits on human spaceflight, including spacecraft, mission artifacts, and interactive simulators.

facts.fact: The Guadalupe River and Frio River are popular tubing and camping destinations in the Texas Hill Country.
facts.background: Cool, spring-fed waters and scenic canyon walls attract families and college students for floating trips, especially in the summer months.

facts.fact: Texas has a long tradition of county courthouses serving as focal points of small-town life.
facts.background: Many county seats feature historic courthouses in central squares, often built in distinctive architectural styles like Romanesque Revival or Classical Revival.

facts.fact: The Texas bluebonnet season depends on rainfall patterns during the previous fall and winter.
facts.background: Good rains at the right time can lead to spectacular wildflower blooms, while drought years may produce fewer blossoms.

facts.fact: Texas has multiple major military installations, including Fort Cavazos, Joint Base San Antonio, and others.
facts.background: These installations support national defense, training, and research, and they contribute significantly to local economies.
facts.source: U.S. Department of Defense base listings

facts.fact: The Piney Woods region of East Texas is characterized by dense forests of pine and hardwood trees.
facts.background: This region contrasts with the grasslands and deserts of West Texas, showcasing the state’s ecological diversity.

facts.fact: The Texas Gulf Coast is a critical stopover for migratory birds following the Central Flyway.
facts.background: Wetlands, bays, and coastal habitats provide feeding and resting sites for millions of birds traveling between North and South America.

facts.fact: Texas public schools are overseen by the Texas Education Agency and State Board of Education.
facts.background: These bodies set curriculum standards, accountability systems, and policies that affect millions of students statewide.

facts.fact: The Texas State Aquarium in Corpus Christi focuses on Gulf of Mexico marine life and conservation.
facts.background: Exhibits highlight coastal ecosystems, sea turtles, sharks, and efforts to rehabilitate injured wildlife.

facts.fact: The Dallas Arts District is one of the largest contiguous urban arts districts in the United States.
facts.background: It includes museums, performance halls, and parks, making Dallas a major center for visual and performing arts.

facts.fact: Texas has both English and Spanish widely spoken in daily life, especially in border and urban areas.
facts.background: Bilingual communities and cultural exchange have shaped Texas’s identity, from food and music to business and education.

facts.fact: The Big Thicket National Preserve protects a uniquely diverse region of East Texas.
facts.background: The preserve contains a mix of forests, swamps, and savannas, earning recognition as a UNESCO biosphere reserve.

facts.fact: The Texas legislative session often produces a large number of proposed bills, but only a fraction become law.
facts.background: Limited-session schedules and committee reviews create a process where many ideas are debated but only some pass both chambers and receive the governor’s signature.

facts.fact: Texas’s identity combines influences from Indigenous nations, Spain, Mexico, the Confederacy, the Republic of Texas, and the United States.
facts.background: These overlapping histories shape the state’s symbols, laws, place names, and cultural traditions today.

facts.fact: Stephen F. Austin is known as the 'Father of Texas.'
facts.background: Stephen F. Austin led the first successful Anglo-American colonization of Texas, bringing hundreds of families to settle in what was then Mexican territory.

facts.fact: Moses Austin obtained the original permission from Mexico to colonize Texas but died before carrying it out.
facts.background: Moses Austin secured an empresario grant from Spanish authorities, and after his death his son Stephen F. Austin fulfilled the plan to bring settlers to Texas.

facts.fact: The Old Three Hundred were the first families officially settled in Texas under Stephen F. Austin’s colony.
facts.background: The Old Three Hundred were a group of roughly 300 families who received land grants in Mexican Texas and became the core of early Anglo-American settlement.

facts.fact: The Texas Declaration of Independence was signed at Washington-on-the-Brazos.
facts.background: Delegates met at Washington-on-the-Brazos to declare Texas independent from Mexico, forming the Republic of Texas.

facts.fact: Sam Houston served as the first elected president of the Republic of Texas.
facts.background: After leading Texian forces to victory at San Jacinto, Sam Houston was elected president of the new republic and later served as governor and U.S. senator.

facts.fact: The Treaties of Velasco attempted to end hostilities between Texas and Mexico after the Battle of San Jacinto.
facts.background: Signed by Santa Anna while captive, the treaties called for Mexican withdrawal and recognition of Texas independence, though Mexico later disputed their validity.

facts.fact: The capital of the Republic of Texas moved several times before settling in Austin.
facts.background: Early capitals included Columbia, Houston, and others before the government chose a permanent capital site that became the city of Austin.

facts.fact: Mirabeau B. Lamar, the second president of the Republic of Texas, promoted public education and expansion.
facts.background: Lamar advocated for a strong Texas Republic, moved the capital to Austin, and set aside land for future public schools and universities.

facts.fact: The Council House Fight in San Antonio strained relations between the Republic of Texas and the Comanche people.
facts.background: A peace negotiation turned violent when Texian officials tried to hold Comanche delegates until captives were released, leading to bloodshed and lasting mistrust.

facts.fact: The Comanche were one of the most powerful Indigenous nations on the Southern Plains.
facts.background: For generations, Comanche bands controlled trade routes, raiding territories, and alliances across what is now Texas, Oklahoma, and beyond.

facts.fact: The Texas Navy protected the young republic’s coastline and commerce in the Gulf of Mexico.
facts.background: Ships of the Texas Navy disrupted Mexican supply lines and helped secure trade routes during and after the Texas Revolution.

facts.fact: Anson Jones was the last president of the Republic of Texas before annexation to the United States.
facts.background: Jones presided over the final years of the republic and oversaw the diplomatic process that led to Texas joining the Union as a state.

facts.fact: Texas was annexed to the United States by a joint resolution of Congress rather than a typical treaty.
facts.background: The annexation of Texas was controversial due to issues of slavery and balance of power, so Congress used a joint resolution that required a simple majority.

facts.fact: The Mexican–American War followed soon after Texas was admitted to the Union.
facts.background: Disputes over the Texas–Mexico border and U.S. expansion led to war, resulting in major territorial gains for the United States.

facts.fact: José Antonio Navarro was a prominent Tejano leader who supported Texas independence.
facts.background: Navarro served in the Texas legislature, signed the Texas Declaration of Independence, and advocated for the rights of Tejanos in the new republic and state.

facts.fact: Tejanos—Texans of Mexican descent—played key roles in the Texas Revolution.
facts.background: Tejano leaders and fighters joined Anglo settlers in opposing centralist policies from Mexico, contributing to key battles and political decisions.

facts.fact: The Battle of Goliad and the subsequent Goliad Massacre deeply influenced Texian resolve.
facts.background: After surrendering, hundreds of Texian prisoners were executed by Mexican forces at Goliad, fueling outrage and the rallying cry 'Remember Goliad.'

facts.fact: James Fannin commanded Texian forces at Goliad before their capture and execution.
facts.background: Fannin’s decision to delay withdrawal from Goliad contributed to his troops’ encirclement and capture by Mexican forces.

facts.fact: The Texas Revolution was influenced by earlier federalist revolts within Mexico.
facts.background: Conflicts between centralist and federalist factions in Mexico, including in other regions, shaped the political climate in which Texas sought independence.

facts.fact: Juan Seguín was a Tejano military leader who fought at the Alamo and at San Jacinto.
facts.background: Juan Seguín helped defend the Alamo before carrying a message through enemy lines and later led Tejano cavalry at San Jacinto.

facts.fact: The Runaway Scrape was a mass civilian evacuation during the Texas Revolution.
facts.background: As Santa Anna’s forces advanced, Texans fled eastward toward the U.S. border, suffering hardship while the Texian army regrouped.

facts.fact: Lorenzo de Zavala served as the first vice president of the Republic of Texas.
facts.background: A former Mexican official and supporter of federalism, de Zavala joined the Texian cause and helped organize the new government.

facts.fact: The Republic of Texas issued its own currency known as 'Texas notes.'
facts.background: The young republic struggled with debt and unstable paper currency, leading to financial difficulties throughout its existence.

facts.fact: The Texas Revolution was partly sparked by disputes over immigration, slavery, and centralization under Santa Anna.
facts.background: Anglo settlers and some Tejanos opposed new restrictions on immigration and feared the loss of local autonomy and economic freedom.

facts.fact: The U.S. Civil War deeply divided Texas, though it joined the Confederacy.
facts.background: Texas voters approved secession, but significant numbers of German Texans and Unionists opposed leaving the United States.

facts.fact: Governor Sam Houston refused to swear loyalty to the Confederacy and was removed from office.
facts.background: Opposing secession, Houston refused to take an oath to the Confederate government and was replaced by a pro-Confederate governor.

facts.fact: The Battle of Palmito Ranch in Texas is considered the last land battle of the U.S. Civil War.
facts.background: Fought near Brownsville, the battle occurred after General Lee’s surrender but before news of the war’s end had fully reached the area.

facts.fact: Quanah Parker was a prominent Comanche leader who later advocated for his people on reservations.
facts.background: The son of Comanche chief Peta Nocona and captured Anglo woman Cynthia Ann Parker, Quanah Parker bridged cultures and worked with U.S. officials after the Red River War.

facts.fact: Cynthia Ann Parker was captured as a child in a Comanche raid and later became the mother of Quanah Parker.
facts.background: Taken from her family in a raid on Fort Parker, she grew up within the Comanche Nation and resisted attempts to return her to Anglo society.

facts.fact: The Red River War marked a major U.S. military campaign against Native nations on the Southern Plains, including in Texas.
facts.background: A series of battles and skirmishes forced many Indigenous groups onto reservations and ended much of their armed resistance in the region.

facts.fact: The Texas Constitution of 1876, still in use today, was written after Reconstruction.
facts.background: The constitution limited state government power, reflected distrust of centralized authority, and has been amended many times.

facts.fact: Barbara Jordan became a national figure for her powerful speeches during the Watergate hearings.
facts.background: As a U.S. representative from Texas, Jordan’s remarks on the Constitution and presidential accountability made a lasting impact.

facts.fact: Ann Richards served as the 45th governor of Texas and was known for her sharp wit.
facts.background: Richards promoted ethics reform, education initiatives, and the appointment of more women and minorities to state positions.

facts.fact: The Texas Equal Rights Amendment was added to the state constitution in the 1970s.
facts.background: The amendment prohibits discrimination based on sex, reflecting broader national movements for gender equality.

facts.fact: Hector P. García founded the American G.I. Forum in Texas to advocate for Hispanic veterans.
facts.background: The organization began in Corpus Christi to address discrimination against Mexican American veterans returning from World War II.

facts.fact: Texas played a major role in the cattle drive era after the Civil War.
facts.background: Cowboys drove Longhorn herds from Texas northward along trails like the Chisholm Trail to railheads in Kansas, shaping Western culture.

facts.fact: The Chisholm Trail carried millions of cattle from Texas to Kansas railheads.
facts.background: Starting near the Texas border, the trail became one of the most famous routes for moving cattle to northern markets.

facts.fact: Richard King, founder of King Ranch, was a key figure in South Texas ranching.
facts.background: King built a vast ranching empire that influenced cattle, land management, and the economy of South Texas.

facts.fact: The Freedmen’s communities established after the Civil War gave formerly enslaved Texans places to own land and build institutions.
facts.background: These settlements developed churches, schools, and businesses, preserving African American culture and resilience in Texas.

facts.fact: Prairie View A&M University and Texas Southern University are two prominent historically Black universities in Texas.
facts.background: These institutions have educated generations of Black Texans and played important roles in civil rights and professional training.

facts.fact: The Texas Supreme Court is the highest civil court in the state.
facts.background: Separate from the Court of Criminal Appeals, the Texas Supreme Court handles civil and juvenile cases of statewide importance.

facts.fact: Juan Nepomuceno Cortina, or Juan Cortina, was a Mexican rancher and military leader involved in border conflicts with Texas.
facts.background: Cortina led raids and rebellions in response to disputes over land, justice, and the treatment of Mexican residents along the Rio Grande.

facts.fact: The Texas Governor’s Mansion in Austin has been the official residence of Texas governors since the 1850s.
facts.background: The Greek Revival-style mansion has survived fires, renovations, and numerous historical events.

facts.fact: The Texas Rangers transitioned from frontier fighters to modern criminal investigators.
facts.background: Over time, the Rangers’ role shifted from border defense and Indian wars to investigating major crimes and supporting local law enforcement.

facts.fact: The Battle of Adobe Walls involved buffalo hunters and Native warriors on the Texas Panhandle plains.
facts.background: Fights near Adobe Walls intensified conflict over the destruction of buffalo herds and Native resistance to settlement.

facts.fact: The Texas Frontier era lasted well into the late 19th century in some regions.
facts.background: Remote areas of West Texas saw conflicts, cattle rustling, and limited law enforcement long after other parts of the United States were more settled.

facts.fact: Oveta Culp Hobby from Texas led the Women’s Army Corps during World War II.
facts.background: Hobby became the first commanding officer of the Women’s Army Corps and later served as the first U.S. Secretary of Health, Education, and Welfare.

facts.fact: Dallas was the site of President John F. Kennedy’s assassination in 1963.
facts.background: Kennedy was shot while riding in a motorcade through Dealey Plaza, an event that left a lasting mark on Dallas and the nation.

facts.fact: The Texas School for the Deaf in Austin is one of the oldest schools for deaf students in the United States.
facts.background: Founded in the 19th century, the school has provided specialized education and advocacy for deaf and hard-of-hearing students in Texas.

facts.fact: The Texas School Book Depository in Dallas now houses the Sixth Floor Museum on the Kennedy assassination.
facts.background: The museum examines the life, death, and legacy of President Kennedy and the historical context of the 1960s.

facts.fact: Jovita Idár was a Mexican American journalist and activist from Texas who advocated for civil rights and education.
facts.background: Idár wrote for Spanish-language newspapers, organized women’s groups, and spoke out against discrimination and violence.

facts.fact: The Texas Centennial Exposition in 1936 celebrated 100 years since independence from Mexico.
facts.background: Held in Dallas, the exposition promoted Texas history, industry, and culture during the Great Depression.

facts.fact: The Alamo Cenotaph in San Antonio honors the defenders who died during the 1836 siege.
facts.background: The monument lists many of the known defenders and serves as a central memorial for visitors to Alamo Plaza.

facts.fact: Judge Roy Bean, the self-styled 'Law West of the Pecos,' held court in a saloon in West Texas.
facts.background: Bean became a frontier legend for his colorful and sometimes unconventional approach to dispensing justice along the Rio Grande.
facts.source: Old West folklore and court records

facts.fact: The Texas State Historical Association was founded in 1897 to preserve and study the state’s history.
facts.background: The association publishes books, articles, and the Handbook of Texas Online, a key reference for Texas history.

facts.fact: Mexican vaquero traditions heavily influenced Texas cowboy culture.
facts.background: Roping techniques, saddles, clothing styles, and ranch terminology in Texas owe much to vaquero practices from northern Mexico.

facts.fact: Lyndon B. Johnson’s 'Great Society' programs had significant impacts in Texas and across the nation.
facts.background: As president from Texas, Johnson championed civil rights laws, Medicare, Medicaid, and anti-poverty initiatives.

facts.fact: The Texas Revolution included smaller skirmishes such as the Battle of Concepción and the Grass Fight near San Antonio.
facts.background: These clashes helped build Texian confidence and weakened Mexican control ahead of the major battles at the Alamo and San Jacinto.

facts.fact: The Convention of 1836 not only declared independence but also drafted a constitution for the Republic of Texas.
facts.background: Delegates at Washington-on-the-Brazos created a framework for government that blended U.S. and Mexican legal traditions.

facts.fact: The Texas German Belt refers to communities of German immigrants who settled in central Texas in the 19th century.
facts.background: Towns like New Braunfels and Fredericksburg preserved German language, customs, and architectural styles that still influence the region today.

facts.fact: Texas experienced a major hurricane and flood known as the 1921 Central Texas Flood.
facts.background: Heavy rains from a tropical system caused catastrophic flooding around San Antonio and Austin, leading to hundreds of deaths.

facts.fact: The Texas oil industry helped fuel Allied efforts during both World Wars.
facts.background: Oil from Texas fields supplied fuel and lubricants for ships, aircraft, and vehicles, contributing significantly to wartime logistics.

facts.fact: The Bracero Program brought Mexican laborers to Texas and other states during World War II and afterward.
facts.background: The program addressed labor shortages in agriculture and railroads, influencing migration patterns and communities along the border.

facts.fact: The Texas gubernatorial office has been held by a variety of figures, including ranchers, lawyers, teachers, and veterans.
facts.background: The diversity of governors reflects changing eras and priorities in Texas politics, from frontier law and order to modern economic development.

facts.fact: José de Escandón helped lead Spanish colonization efforts in South Texas and northeastern Mexico in the 18th century.
facts.background: Escandón founded settlements and missions along the lower Rio Grande, laying groundwork for later communities.

facts.fact: The Texas Negro League and other Black baseball teams flourished during segregation.
facts.background: African American players and teams in Texas contributed to a rich baseball culture long before the integration of Major League Baseball.

facts.fact: Texas cities such as Houston and Dallas became major centers for the civil rights movement in the mid-20th century.
facts.background: Sit-ins, boycotts, and legal challenges in Texas helped dismantle segregation in schools, transportation, and public accommodations.

facts.fact: James S. Hogg, elected in 1890, was the first native-born governor of Texas.
facts.background: Hogg promoted railroad regulation and other reforms, reflecting the rise of populist politics in the late 19th century.

facts.fact: Tejano music pioneers from Texas helped create modern conjunto and Tejano styles.
facts.background: Musicians blended Mexican, German, Czech, and American influences to develop distinctive Texas borderland sounds.

facts.fact: The Texas Historical Commission oversees official state historical markers and preservation programs.
facts.background: Thousands of roadside markers and designated sites across Texas tell local stories about people, events, and places.

facts.fact: Many Texas counties are named after heroes of the Texas Revolution and early state leaders.
facts.background: Names like Houston, Travis, Fannin, and Lamar reflect the legacy of individuals tied to independence and early governance.

facts.fact: The Texas folktale of Pecos Bill is a larger-than-life cowboy legend rooted in Western storytelling.
facts.background: Although fictional, Pecos Bill stories reflect frontier humor and exaggeration that became part of Texas and Western folklore.

facts.fact: The 1901 Spindletop oil discovery near Beaumont transformed many ordinary Texans into oil boom entrepreneurs.
facts.background: Investors, speculators, and workers flooded the area as gushers created sudden wealth and rapid industrialization.

facts.fact: The Texas State Teachers Association formed in the 19th century to advocate for public education and teachers.
facts.background: The organization pushed for better schools, higher standards, and improved pay and training for educators.

facts.fact: The Karankawa people once lived along the Gulf Coast of Texas.
facts.background: Karankawa bands fished, hunted, and traveled by canoe, but disease, warfare, and displacement later devastated their communities.

facts.fact: The Caddo people built complex village communities in East Texas long before European contact.
facts.background: Caddo societies practiced agriculture, mound-building, and extensive trade, influencing the cultural landscape of the region.

facts.fact: Texas missions were part of Spain’s effort to extend its empire and convert Indigenous peoples to Christianity.
facts.background: Mission sites in San Antonio, Goliad, and East Texas combined religious instruction, farming, and military functions.

facts.fact: Many Texas towns grew up along railroad lines built in the late 19th century.
facts.background: Rail connections linked rural communities to markets and cities, shaping patterns of settlement and economic development.

facts.fact: Texas produced notable writers such as Katherine Anne Porter, Larry McMurtry, and Sandra Cisneros.
facts.background: These authors explored themes of Texas life, borderlands, family, and identity in works that gained national recognition.

facts.fact: The Great Depression hit Texas farmers and oil workers hard, but New Deal programs helped build infrastructure and conservation projects.
facts.background: Projects like soil conservation, flood control, and public buildings left lasting marks on the Texas landscape.

facts.fact: The Brown v. Board of Education decision led to desegregation struggles in Texas schools.
facts.background: Local officials and communities responded in different ways, with court orders and activism eventually dismantling legal segregation in education.

facts.fact: Jane McCallum of Texas was a leading suffragist who helped win women’s right to vote.
facts.background: She organized rallies, wrote editorials, and later served in state government, promoting civic engagement and reform.

facts.fact: Texas women won the right to vote in state primaries before the 19th Amendment was ratified.
facts.background: A state law allowed women to vote in primary elections, an important step toward full suffrage.

facts.fact: The term 'Lone Star State' harkens back to the single star on the Republic of Texas flag.
facts.background: The lone star symbolizes independence and unity, and it remains central to Texas identity and iconography.

facts.fact: Mexican and Texan forces clashed at the 1832 Battle of Velasco before full revolution broke out.
facts.background: The conflict at Velasco was part of growing tensions over customs duties and local autonomy under Mexican rule.

facts.fact: The 1835 Consultation in San Felipe de Austin debated whether Texans were fighting for Mexican federalism or full independence.
facts.background: Delegates initially framed their struggle as supporting Mexico’s federal constitution, but the movement soon moved toward independence.

facts.fact: Many enslaved African Americans were brought to Texas during the Republic and early statehood periods.
facts.background: Slavery expanded rapidly in Texas as cotton and plantation agriculture grew, shaping demographics and politics until emancipation.

facts.fact: Texas’s population has included long-established communities of Czech, Polish, and other Central European immigrants.
facts.background: These groups created towns, churches, and cultural traditions that remain visible in festivals, foods, and architecture.

facts.fact: Carlos Truan of Corpus Christi was a major Hispanic legislative leader in the Texas Senate.
facts.background: Truan advocated for bilingual education, social services, and the interests of South Texas communities for decades.

facts.fact: Mexican American farm workers in Texas played key roles in the broader farm labor movement.
facts.background: Organizing efforts and strikes raised awareness of low wages, poor conditions, and the need for protections for migrant laborers.

facts.fact: Thurgood Marshall argued important civil rights cases involving Texas before the U.S. Supreme Court.
facts.background: Cases such as Smith v. Allwright challenged white-only primaries, reshaping voter rights and racial politics in Texas and beyond.

facts.fact: Selena Quintanilla-Pérez helped bring Tejano music to mainstream audiences before her death in 1995.
facts.background: Her career and legacy made her an enduring cultural icon in Texas and across the Spanish-speaking world.

facts.fact: Texas has been home to multiple Nobel Prize recipients in fields like physics, chemistry, and medicine.
facts.background: Researchers at Texas universities and institutions have contributed groundbreaking work recognized with international awards.

facts.fact: The rise of the Texas aerospace industry brought new technology and jobs to cities like Houston and Dallas–Fort Worth.
facts.background: Defense contracts, NASA projects, and private companies helped create a significant high-tech sector in the state.

facts.fact: The 1957 drought in Texas was one of the worst in modern history and spurred new water planning efforts.
facts.background: Severe water shortages led to reservoir construction, conservation measures, and long-term planning for a growing population.
facts.source: Texas drought and water policy histories

facts.fact: The Republic of Texas operated its own diplomatic missions and signed treaties with other nations.
facts.background: Texas sought recognition and support from countries such as France, Britain, and the United States during its years as an independent nation.

facts.fact: The Texas Archives War was a dispute over the location of the republic’s government records.
facts.background: Citizens of Austin resisted efforts to move archives to Houston, reflecting tensions over the capital’s location and political power.

facts.fact: Many Texas towns were named for early settlers, national heroes, or geographic features.
facts.background: Names like Houston, Austin, Laredo, and Nacogdoches reflect Indigenous, Spanish, Mexican, and Anglo influences.

facts.fact: Texas’s demographic history includes waves of migration from other U.S. states and from countries around the world.
facts.background: Oil booms, military bases, economic opportunities, and refugee resettlement programs have all shaped who calls Texas home.

facts.fact: The story of Texas history is often summarized as a blend of Indigenous nations, Spanish colonization, Mexican governance, independence, and U.S. statehood.
facts.background: These overlapping eras created a complex legacy of laws, languages, and cultural traditions that continue to define modern Texas.

facts.fact: The Texas Panhandle is characterized by wide-open plains and big skies.
facts.background: Located in the northernmost part of the state, the Panhandle includes flat high plains, agriculture, and ranching, with weather that can change rapidly.

facts.fact: Amarillo is a major city in the Texas Panhandle and a historic stop along Route 66.
facts.background: Amarillo grew as a cattle, railroad, and later energy center, and today it attracts travelers with roadside attractions and nearby Palo Duro Canyon.

facts.fact: The South Plains region of Texas is a leading producer of cotton.
facts.background: Centered around Lubbock, the South Plains uses irrigation from the Ogallala Aquifer to support large cotton fields and other crops.

facts.fact: Lubbock is often called the 'Hub City' of West Texas.
facts.background: Lubbock serves as a commercial, educational, and health-care hub for smaller towns across the South Plains and surrounding rural areas.

facts.fact: The High Plains of Texas form part of the larger Great Plains region.
facts.background: High, flat plateaus with fertile soils support grain, cotton, and cattle, while strong winds make the area suitable for wind energy.

facts.fact: The Gulf Coast region of Texas stretches from Sabine Pass to the Rio Grande.
facts.background: This region includes beaches, bays, marshes, barrier islands, and major port cities like Houston, Corpus Christi, and Brownsville.

facts.fact: Corpus Christi is known as the 'Sparkling City by the Sea.'
facts.background: Overlooking Corpus Christi Bay, the city serves as a port, petrochemical center, naval aviation hub, and coastal vacation destination.

facts.fact: The Rio Grande Valley, often called 'The Valley,' is a subtropical region at the southern tip of Texas.
facts.background: Cities like Brownsville, McAllen, Harlingen, and Edinburg form a fast-growing border region with strong ties to Mexico and rich agricultural production.

facts.fact: The Hill Country is known for rolling limestone hills, clear rivers, and small towns.
facts.background: Located in central Texas, the Hill Country includes communities like Fredericksburg, Kerrville, and Wimberley, popular for outdoor recreation and scenic drives.

facts.fact: Fredericksburg in the Hill Country reflects strong German cultural influences.
facts.background: Founded by German settlers in the 19th century, the town features German-style architecture, bakeries, and festivals.

facts.fact: The Balcones Canyonlands in Central Texas contain deep canyons and unique wildlife.
facts.background: This Hill Country area supports endangered species such as the golden-cheeked warbler and black-capped vireo.
facts.source: Balcones Canyonlands National Wildlife Refuge information

facts.fact: The Piney Woods of East Texas are dominated by loblolly and shortleaf pine forests.
facts.background: High rainfall, acidic soils, and dense forests distinguish this region from the prairies and deserts found elsewhere in the state.

facts.fact: Tyler, in East Texas, is known as the 'Rose Capital of America.'
facts.background: The city’s rose-growing industry and annual Texas Rose Festival highlight local horticulture and traditions.

facts.fact: The Big Thicket region of Southeast Texas contains an unusual mix of forest types.
facts.background: Pine forests, hardwood bottomlands, and bogs converge in the Big Thicket, supporting high biodiversity in a relatively small area.

facts.fact: Dallas–Fort Worth sits in a region often called the 'Prairies and Lakes' of North-Central Texas.
facts.background: Originally tallgrass prairies and oak woodlands, the area now includes extensive reservoirs, suburbs, and major business centers.

facts.fact: North Texas experiences more dramatic temperature swings than the Gulf Coast due to its distance from the ocean.
facts.background: With fewer moderating coastal influences, North Texas can see hot summers, cold winter blasts, and severe thunderstorms.

facts.fact: El Paso anchors the far western tip of Texas along the Rio Grande.
facts.background: The city forms a binational metro area with Ciudad Juárez, Mexico, and is culturally tied to both the American Southwest and northern Mexico.

facts.fact: Far West Texas includes desert landscapes, mountain ranges, and remote ranchlands.
facts.background: This sparsely populated region contains the Davis Mountains, Big Bend, and wide stretches of Chihuahuan Desert.

facts.fact: The Davis Mountains in West Texas are sometimes called the 'Texas Alps.'
facts.background: Cooler temperatures, higher elevations, and forested slopes make the Davis Mountains a unique sky island amid the desert.
facts.source: Davis Mountains park and tourism information

facts.fact: Marfa sits on a high plateau in the Trans-Pecos region of West Texas.
facts.background: The town is known for its minimalist art scene, desert landscapes, and mysterious 'Marfa Lights.'

facts.fact: Big Bend National Park preserves a remote bend of the Rio Grande in West Texas.
facts.background: The park includes desert, river, and mountain environments, with Chisos Mountains peaks rising above the Chihuahuan Desert floor.

facts.fact: Big Bend Ranch State Park, just west of Big Bend National Park, is Texas’s largest state park.
facts.background: This rugged park offers backcountry hiking, off-road driving, and Rio Grande access in a remote desert setting.

facts.fact: The Coastal Bend region includes bays and barrier islands such as Mustang Island and Padre Island.
facts.background: Fishing, birding, and beach tourism thrive in this part of South Texas, which also supports shipping and energy industries.

facts.fact: South Padre Island is a popular beach destination at the southern end of the Texas coast.
facts.background: Known for sandy beaches, windsurfing, and spring break crowds, South Padre also provides nesting habitat for sea turtles and shorebirds.

facts.fact: Galveston Island sits on the upper Texas coast and has a long history as a port and resort.
facts.background: Before hurricanes shifted commerce inland, Galveston was a leading Gulf port; today it is known for tourism and cruise ship terminals.

facts.fact: The Houston metropolitan area lies in the Gulf Coast Prairie region.
facts.background: Built on former prairie, marsh, and forest lands, Greater Houston includes bayous, ship channels, and sprawling suburban developments.

facts.fact: The Rio Grande forms the international border between Texas and Mexico for roughly 1,200 miles.
facts.background: The river’s course shapes communities, agriculture, and wildlife habitats from El Paso to the Gulf of Mexico.

facts.fact: The Edwards Plateau underlies much of the Hill Country and influences its springs and rivers.
facts.background: Limestone bedrock and karst features help create clear, spring-fed rivers like the Blanco, San Marcos, and Guadalupe.

facts.fact: Central Texas rivers such as the Guadalupe and Llano are popular for tubing, kayaking, and fishing.
facts.background: Cool, clear waters and scenic banks make these rivers key recreation sites for residents of nearby towns and cities.

facts.fact: The Rolling Plains region lies between the High Plains and more wooded areas of North and Central Texas.
facts.background: Rolling grasslands, mesquite, and scattered canyons support ranching, hunting, and small farming communities.

facts.fact: Wichita Falls is a key city in the Rolling Plains region of North Texas.
facts.background: The city grew around ranching, oil, and military installations and today serves as a regional commercial hub.

facts.fact: The Cross Timbers region marks a transition between eastern forests and western prairies.
facts.background: Oak woodlands and prairies in North-Central Texas historically formed a natural barrier between different ecosystems.

facts.fact: The Blackland Prairie once stretched through parts of North and Central Texas with deep, fertile soils.
facts.background: Much of this prairie has been converted to farms and urban areas, making remnant patches important for conservation.

facts.fact: The Rio Grande Valley’s warm climate supports citrus groves and winter vegetable crops.
facts.background: Grapefruits, oranges, and other produce have long been important to the Valley’s agricultural economy.

facts.fact: Brownsville, at the mouth of the Rio Grande, is one of the southernmost cities in Texas.
facts.background: The city has a rich borderland culture and a history tied to trade, conflict, and migration between the U.S. and Mexico.

facts.fact: The Lower Rio Grande Valley is one of the best bird-watching regions in North America.
facts.background: Tropical and migratory bird species reach the northern edge of their range here, drawing birders from around the world.
facts.source: Birding guides for the Lower Rio Grande Valley

facts.fact: The Panhandle’s Palo Duro Canyon is sometimes called the 'Grand Canyon of Texas.'
facts.background: Steep walls, colorful rock layers, and dramatic vistas make the canyon a major outdoor destination for hiking, camping, and sightseeing.
facts.source: Palo Duro Canyon State Park information

facts.fact: The Llano Estacado is a vast, high plateau that covers parts of West Texas and eastern New Mexico.
facts.background: Meaning 'Staked Plains,' the region is known for its flatness, big skies, and historic challenges for travelers seeking landmarks.

facts.fact: Central Texas is sometimes called the 'Heart of Texas' due to its location and political significance.
facts.background: With Austin as the state capital and a growing tech sector, Central Texas has become an influential region in state affairs.

facts.fact: San Antonio sits at the edge of the Hill Country and South Texas Plains.
facts.background: The city’s location combines influences from Spanish missions, German settlers, ranching, and modern urban development.

facts.fact: The South Texas Plains region transitions from brushland to coastal prairies and the Rio Grande Valley.
facts.background: Mesquite, thorny shrubs, and grasslands dominate this region, supporting cattle ranching and wildlife.

facts.fact: Kingsville, near the South Texas Plains and Coastal Bend, is closely associated with King Ranch.
facts.background: The town developed alongside ranching, railroads, and later higher education at Texas A&M University–Kingsville.

facts.fact: The Sabine River forms part of the boundary between Texas and Louisiana in East Texas.
facts.background: Flowing through forested landscapes and reservoirs, the Sabine separates Texas from its eastern neighbor and supports recreation and industry.

facts.fact: Lake Texoma, on the Red River, is one of the largest reservoirs in the United States.
facts.background: Straddling the Texas–Oklahoma border, Lake Texoma is popular for boating, fishing, and camping.
facts.source: U.S. Army Corps of Engineers lake information

facts.fact: The Red River marks much of the northern border between Texas and Oklahoma.
facts.background: Historically disputed, the river’s shifting course influenced legal battles and boundary surveys between the two states.

facts.fact: Texarkana is a twin city that straddles the border between Texas and Arkansas.
facts.background: With one city in Texas and one in Arkansas, Texarkana serves as a regional hub for both states.

facts.fact: The city of Beaumont lies in the Golden Triangle region of Southeast Texas.
facts.background: Beaumont, Port Arthur, and Orange form an industrial and shipping area tied to oil refining and petrochemicals.

facts.fact: The Golden Triangle region played a key role in the early Texas oil industry.
facts.background: Spindletop and nearby fields turned Beaumont-area communities into boomtowns and reshaped energy markets.

facts.fact: Abilene lies in the North-Central region where the Rolling Plains meet West Texas.
facts.background: Founded as a railroad town, Abilene became a regional center for education, health care, and commerce.

facts.fact: The Concho Valley region around San Angelo features rivers and live oak savannas.
facts.background: Named for the Concho (or 'shell') River, the area blends ranching, military history, and small-city life.

facts.fact: The Permian Basin, spanning West Texas and southeastern New Mexico, is one of the most productive oil regions in North America.
facts.background: Oil and gas extraction in the Permian has fueled local growth and has national and global energy impacts.

facts.fact: Midland and Odessa are twin cities at the heart of the Permian Basin oil fields.
facts.background: These West Texas cities reflect oil boom-and-bust cycles, with economies centered on energy, services, and logistics.

facts.fact: The Panhandle’s Caprock Escarpment forms a dramatic cliff marking the edge of the High Plains.
facts.background: The escarpment drops sharply into lower plains, creating scenic overlooks and canyons.

facts.fact: Caprock Canyons State Park in the Panhandle protects striking red rock formations and bison habitat.
facts.background: The park is home to the official Texas State Bison Herd, descended from remnants saved by ranchers in the late 1800s.

facts.fact: The Brazos River flows from the High Plains through Central Texas to the Gulf of Mexico.
facts.background: One of the longest rivers in Texas, the Brazos has supported agriculture, transportation, and towns along its course.

facts.fact: The Colorado River of Texas runs entirely within the state before reaching the Gulf.
facts.background: Originating in the Hill Country, the river passes through Austin and forms a series of reservoirs known as the Highland Lakes.

facts.fact: The Highland Lakes region northwest of Austin is a popular boating and fishing destination.
facts.background: Reservoirs such as Lake Travis, Lake LBJ, and Lake Buchanan provide water supply, recreation, and hydroelectric power.
facts.source: Highland Lakes tourism and utility resources

facts.fact: East Texas’s lakes and forests support a major timber industry.
facts.background: Pine plantations and mixed forests provide lumber, paper products, and wood-based manufacturing jobs.

facts.fact: The Sabine–Neches Waterway near Beaumont is a major shipping channel for petrochemical products.
facts.background: Refineries and chemical plants along this waterway make the region a key part of the global energy supply chain.

facts.fact: Kerrville, in the Hill Country, is known for its scenic river valley and arts community.
facts.background: Kerrville hosts music festivals, art events, and outdoor activities along the Guadalupe River.

facts.fact: The Llano River in the Hill Country is famous for granite outcrops and spring-fed pools.
facts.background: Popular for paddling and fishing, the river flows through scenic ranchlands before joining the Colorado River.

facts.fact: The Guadalupe Peak trail in West Texas leads to the highest point in the state.
facts.background: Hikers climb over 3,000 feet from the trailhead to reach the summit in Guadalupe Mountains National Park.

facts.fact: The Pine Curtain is an informal name for the thick forests separating East Texas from other regions.
facts.background: Dense pine stands historically made travel difficult and contributed to East Texas’s distinct identity.

facts.fact: College Station, in the Brazos Valley region, is home to Texas A&M University.
facts.background: The university dominates local culture and economy, attracting students and researchers from around the world.

facts.fact: The Brazos Valley region includes cities like Bryan, College Station, and Navasota.
facts.background: The area blends agriculture, higher education, and technology industries along the Brazos River corridor.

facts.fact: The Metroplex region includes Dallas, Fort Worth, Arlington, and dozens of surrounding cities.
facts.background: This North Texas urban area has grown into one of the largest metropolitan regions in the country.

facts.fact: Fort Worth promotes itself as 'Where the West Begins.'
facts.background: Historic stockyards, rodeos, and Western-themed districts reflect its cattle, railroad, and military heritage.

facts.fact: The Dallas–Fort Worth region sits atop parts of the Barnett Shale natural gas formation.
facts.background: Barnett Shale development in the early 2000s spurred drilling, pipelines, and debates over urban energy production.

facts.fact: The Panhandle and High Plains region often experiences strong winds ideal for wind farms.
facts.background: Turbines across West Texas feed electricity into the state’s grid, making Texas a national leader in wind power.

facts.fact: The Hill Country wine region has grown into a major attraction for wine tourism.
facts.background: Dozens of vineyards and tasting rooms line highways near Fredericksburg, Johnson City, and other towns.

facts.fact: The Big Country is a nickname for the region around Abilene and the central West Texas plains.
facts.background: The term reflects the expansive ranchlands, open horizons, and rugged landscapes characteristic of the area.

facts.fact: South Texas brush country supports white-tailed deer, javelina, and many game birds.
facts.background: Ranchlands and hunting leases in this region attract hunters from across Texas and beyond.

facts.fact: The Texas Coastal Prairie once extended inland from the Gulf Coast, but much has been converted to farms and cities.
facts.background: Remaining prairies are important for migratory birds, pollinators, and flood mitigation.

facts.fact: The Sabine National Forest and Angelina National Forest protect parts of the Piney Woods in East Texas.
facts.background: These national forests provide recreation, wildlife habitat, and timber resources.

facts.fact: The Trans-Pecos region of West Texas includes some of the state’s highest elevations and driest climates.
facts.background: Mountains, desert basins, and isolated towns give the region a remote, frontier feeling.

facts.fact: The Pecos River flows through arid country before joining the Rio Grande at the Texas–Mexico border.
facts.background: Steep canyon walls and reservoirs like Lake Amistad make the river a striking feature of West Texas.

facts.fact: Lake Amistad near Del Rio is a large reservoir shared by the United States and Mexico.
facts.background: The lake is known for clear waters, fishing, and rock art sites in surrounding canyons.

facts.fact: Del Rio sits on the Rio Grande in a region of springs, fisheries, and border culture.
facts.background: Nearby Lake Amistad and archaeological sites highlight the area’s natural and human history.

facts.fact: The Gulf Coast’s bays and estuaries provide crucial nursery habitat for shrimp, fish, and other marine life.
facts.background: Healthy estuaries support both commercial fishing and recreational angling along the Texas coast.

facts.fact: Port Aransas on Mustang Island is known for saltwater fishing and bird-watching.
facts.background: The town hosts fishing tournaments and lies along major bird migration routes on the Central Flyway.
facts.source: Port Aransas tourism and wildlife guides

facts.fact: The Upper Coast region around Houston and Galveston is more industrialized than the Middle and Lower Coast.
facts.background: Refineries, chemical plants, and ports dominate much of the Upper Coast economy.

facts.fact: The Middle Coast includes Matagorda Bay, San Antonio Bay, and Aransas Bay.
facts.background: These bays host important fisheries, bird rookeries, and coastal communities with strong ties to the sea.

facts.fact: The Lower Coast near the Rio Grande is warmer and more subtropical than the Upper Coast.
facts.background: Palms, semi-tropical vegetation, and year-round birdlife distinguish the Lower Coast region.

facts.fact: The Hill Country’s limestone caves include popular sites such as Natural Bridge Caverns.
facts.background: These caves showcase stalactites, stalagmites, and underground streams carved out by groundwater over time.
facts.source: Texas cave tourism and geology sources

facts.fact: The Panhandle’s wide vistas make it an excellent place to view sunsets and stargaze.
facts.background: With fewer trees and relatively low light pollution, the region offers expansive views of the sky.

facts.fact: The Hill Country is known for small-town courthouse squares and historic main streets.
facts.background: Towns like Llano, Mason, and Blanco preserve stone courthouses and businesses around central squares.

facts.fact: The Panhandle’s Canadian River cuts through canyons and mesas before flowing into Oklahoma.
facts.background: Lake Meredith on the Canadian River provides water and recreation for surrounding communities.

facts.fact: The Gulf Coast’s barrier islands help protect inland bays and marshes from storm waves.
facts.background: Islands like Galveston, Mustang, and Padre absorb storm energy and shape coastal erosion and deposition patterns.

facts.fact: The El Paso region lies in the Chihuahuan Desert, one of North America’s major desert ecosystems.
facts.background: Desert plants such as creosote, agave, and yucca thrive in the hot, dry climate around El Paso.

facts.fact: The Hill Country’s elevation and rocky terrain support unique plant communities, including Ashe juniper and live oak woodlands.
facts.background: These woodlands provide habitat for endangered songbirds and other wildlife.

facts.fact: East Texas’s bayous and bottomland forests are home to alligators, wading birds, and diverse fish species.
facts.background: Wetlands such as those along the Neches and Trinity rivers support rich biodiversity.

facts.fact: The Panhandle’s winters can bring snow and ice, unlike the milder winters along the Gulf Coast.
facts.background: Higher elevation and distance from the ocean lead to colder winter conditions in the Panhandle.

facts.fact: Central Texas bluebonnet and wildflower drives attract visitors each spring.
facts.background: Roads near towns like Burnet, Ennis, and Brenham are famous for colorful roadside blooms.

facts.fact: The small towns of the Rio Grande Valley each have their own unique blend of Mexican and American culture.
facts.background: Local festivals, foods, and traditions celebrate borderland identity in communities like San Juan, Pharr, and Mission.

facts.fact: South Texas ranchlands can be vast, covering tens of thousands of acres of brush country.
facts.background: These ranches support cattle, wildlife, and sometimes oil and gas production.

facts.fact: East Texas towns such as Nacogdoches and Lufkin serve as forest and education centers.
facts.background: Nacogdoches is home to Stephen F. Austin State University, while surrounding forests support logging and recreation.

facts.fact: The Panhandle’s towns often developed around railroad lines and grain elevators.
facts.background: Communities like Hereford, Pampa, and Dumas reflect agricultural and transportation roots.

facts.fact: North Texas suburbs such as Plano, Frisco, and McKinney have grown rapidly in recent decades.
facts.background: These cities evolved from small towns into major residential and commercial centers within the Dallas–Fort Worth metro area.

facts.fact: The Gulf Coast’s fishing culture ranges from commercial shrimping to recreational bay and offshore fishing.
facts.background: Communities like Port Isabel, Port Aransas, and Freeport blend working waterfronts with tourism-focused marinas.

facts.fact: Regional diversity is one of Texas’s defining features, with each part of the state offering distinct landscapes, economies, and cultures.
facts.background: From the forests of East Texas to the deserts of West Texas, and from the Panhandle plains to the Gulf Coast, Texans experience many different versions of their state.

facts.fact: The State Fair of Texas in Dallas is famous for its fried foods, including fried Oreos and fried butter.
facts.background: Vendors at the State Fair compete each year to create new and unusual fried dishes, turning the fair into a showcase of creative—and sometimes outrageous—Texas cuisine.
facts.source: State Fair of Texas food features

facts.fact: Big Tex, the 55-foot-tall cowboy at the State Fair of Texas, once caught fire in 2012.
facts.background: An electrical malfunction caused Big Tex to burn, but he was later rebuilt and returned to the fair the next year with updated features.

facts.fact: The small town of Round Top hosts one of the largest antique shows in the country.
facts.background: Several times a year, Round Top and surrounding communities fill with vendors, collectors, and shoppers hunting for vintage and antique treasures.

facts.fact: Luckenbach, Texas, is famous for its country music dance hall and tiny post office.
facts.background: Immortalized in a Waylon Jennings song, Luckenbach is a Hill Country hamlet where people gather for live music, dancing, and a laid-back atmosphere.

facts.fact: Austin’s unofficial motto is 'Keep Austin Weird.'
facts.background: The phrase reflects the city’s appreciation for creativity, local businesses, and quirky traditions, and appears on bumper stickers and T-shirts around town.

facts.fact: The Austin City Limits Music Festival draws major artists and huge crowds to Zilker Park.
facts.background: What began as a TV show about live music grew into a multi-day outdoor festival that attracts fans from around the world.

facts.fact: South by Southwest (SXSW) in Austin combines film, music, and technology conferences.
facts.background: SXSW has launched music careers, tech startups, and movie premieres, turning downtown Austin into a global creative hub each spring.

facts.fact: The Marfa Lights are mysterious glowing orbs reported near the West Texas town of Marfa.
facts.background: For more than a century, locals and visitors have seen unexplained lights on the horizon, inspiring everything from scientific studies to ghost stories.

facts.fact: Cadillac Ranch near Amarillo features classic cars buried nose-first in the ground.
facts.background: Created as a public art installation, the row of half-buried Cadillacs invites visitors to spray-paint the cars and take photos along old Route 66.

facts.fact: The 'World’s Largest Pecan' statue can be found in Seguin, Texas.
facts.background: Seguin, a town known for pecan orchards, built a giant pecan monument as a playful symbol of local agriculture.

facts.fact: There is a giant squirrel statue holding a pecan in Cedar Creek, Texas.
facts.background: The squirrel statue, named 'Ms. Pearl,' adds another quirky landmark to Texas’s collection of oversized roadside icons.

facts.fact: The West Texas town of Slaton hosts an annual '1910 Harvey House' event at its restored railroad hotel.
facts.background: The event celebrates the railroad era when Harvey House restaurants and hotels served travelers along major rail lines.

facts.fact: The Texas Renaissance Festival near Houston is one of the largest Renaissance fairs in the United States.
facts.background: Visitors stroll through a themed village with costumed performers, jousts, and vendors selling crafts, turkey legs, and mead.

facts.fact: Wurstfest in New Braunfels is a popular German-style sausage festival.
facts.background: The event features polka music, traditional foods, carnival rides, and beer, reflecting the area’s German Texan heritage.

facts.fact: The town of Terlingua holds an annual chili cook-off in the desert near Big Bend.
facts.background: Chili enthusiasts gather to compete, camp, and enjoy live music in an isolated West Texas ghost town setting.

facts.fact: Luling is famous for its Watermelon Thump festival.
facts.background: The festival features watermelon seed-spitting contests, parades, and live entertainment celebrating the local watermelon crop.

facts.fact: The tiny town of Dublin, Texas, once promoted itself as the 'Irish Capital of Texas.'
facts.background: Dublin held St. Patrick’s Day events and was long associated with a historic bottling plant for a popular soft drink.

facts.fact: In West, Texas—not to be confused with West Texas—you’ll find a strong Czech Texan culture.
facts.background: Bakeries, kolaches, and festivals in West highlight Czech heritage and attract travelers along Interstate 35.

facts.fact: Many Texas towns hold annual 'Founders Day' or heritage celebrations with parades and barbeque.
