facts.background: These events honor local history, bring communities together, and often feature rodeos, dances, and fireworks.

facts.fact: The Houston Art Car Parade showcases vehicles turned into rolling works of art.
facts.background: Participants cover cars, trucks, and bikes with sculptures, paint, and decorations, transforming everyday vehicles into imaginative creations.

facts.fact: Austin’s Congress Avenue Bridge is home to one of the largest urban bat colonies in the world.
facts.background: Hundreds of thousands of Mexican free-tailed bats fly out from under the bridge at dusk in warm months, drawing crowds of spectators.

facts.fact: The city of Paris, Texas, has its own Eiffel Tower topped with a red cowboy hat.
facts.background: The playful landmark acknowledges the town’s French namesake while adding a Texas twist.

facts.fact: In La Grange, you can find the Texas Czech Heritage and Cultural Center celebrating Czech Texan traditions.
facts.background: The center hosts events, exhibits, and recordings that preserve the language, music, and stories of Czech immigrants and their descendants.

facts.fact: The small town of Yoakum bills itself as the 'Leather Capital of Texas.'
facts.background: Leather production and related industries have been important to Yoakum’s economy and identity.

facts.fact: There is a giant Sam Houston statue along Interstate 45 near Huntsville.
facts.background: Standing over 60 feet tall, the white statue of Sam Houston greets travelers and honors the Texas hero’s legacy.

facts.fact: The town of Hico claims to be the final home of outlaw Billy the Kid, though historians debate the story.
facts.background: Hico’s museum and local legends suggest Billy the Kid lived into old age in Texas under a different name.

facts.fact: Many Texas towns have colorful nicknames, like 'Cowtown' for Fort Worth and 'Space City' for Houston.
facts.background: These nicknames reflect key aspects of local history, such as cattle drives, aerospace, or major industries.

facts.fact: The 'Come and Take It' cannon in Gonzales is commemorated each year at the Come and Take It Festival.
facts.background: Parades, reenactments, and fairs celebrate the 1835 standoff when Texians refused to return a cannon to Mexican forces.

facts.fact: The Texas town of Bowie has a large statue of a Bowie knife at its entrance.
facts.background: Named after Alamo defender James Bowie, the town embraced the iconic knife as a symbol of its frontier heritage.

facts.fact: The 'World’s Largest Cowboy Boots' stand outside a shopping center in San Antonio.
facts.background: The towering art installation of giant boots is a popular photo spot for visitors and locals alike.

facts.fact: Thousands of people attend the Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo in western wear each year.
facts.background: Cowboy hats, boots, and belt buckles fill the stadium as attendees celebrate rodeo competitions, concerts, and carnival rides.
facts.source: Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo attendance reports

facts.fact: The city of San Antonio hosts Fiesta, a multi-day spring festival with parades, food, and music.
facts.background: Fiesta began as a way to honor the memory of the Alamo and San Jacinto and has grown into a citywide celebration.

facts.fact: During Fiesta, San Antonio has a tradition of 'Battle of Flowers' parades led primarily by women.
facts.background: The Battle of Flowers Parade started in the 19th century and remains a centerpiece of Fiesta celebrations.

facts.fact: The small town of Schulenburg is known for its painted churches built by German and Czech immigrants.
facts.background: Plain exteriors hide ornate, colorful interiors that blend European church art with Texas rural life.

facts.fact: The West Texas town of Alpine hosts the Viva Big Bend music festival.
facts.background: Bands perform in venues across multiple towns, showcasing regional musicians and the wide-open atmosphere of Far West Texas.

facts.fact: The town of Giddings holds an annual 'Crawfish Festival' celebrating Cajun and Texas flavors.
facts.background: Crawfish boils, live music, and family activities bring Gulf Coast influences into Central Texas.

facts.fact: The quirky cathedral of junk in Austin is a towering backyard sculpture made from recycled materials.
facts.background: Built by an artist over many years, it has become an offbeat landmark embodying Austin’s creative spirit.

facts.fact: The Donut Stop in Amarillo offers a local favorite shaped like a Texas map.
facts.background: Texas-shaped foods—from waffles to donuts—are a playful way to show state pride at breakfast.

facts.fact: Texas State University in San Marcos has a tradition of students floating the San Marcos River between classes and on weekends.
facts.background: Tubing and swimming in the spring-fed river have become part of campus life and local recreation.

facts.fact: The 'Beer Can House' in Houston is a folk art landmark covered in flattened beer cans and pull tabs.
facts.background: Created by a homeowner over decades, the house reflects an individual vision and has been preserved as a museum piece.

facts.fact: The tiny town of Dime Box is often mentioned in Texas jokes and tall tales for its unusual name.
facts.background: The name reportedly came from a box used to collect dime postage for mail delivery in the late 1800s.

facts.fact: In some Texas towns, high school football games feel like major community events with large crowds and marching bands.
facts.background: Friday night lights in Texas symbolize local pride, school spirit, and friendly rivalry between neighboring towns.

facts.fact: The small city of Brenham is known for both bluebonnets and a popular ice cream brand.
facts.background: Brenham blends scenic drives through wildflowers with tours and tastes at a well-known ice cream creamery.

facts.fact: The town of Poteet claims the title 'Strawberry Capital of Texas' and hosts an annual strawberry festival.
facts.background: The festival features pageants, music, and plenty of fresh strawberries and strawberry-themed foods.

facts.fact: The 'Weiner Dog Races' in Buda, Texas, feature dachshunds sprinting down a track.
facts.background: The playful event draws spectators from across Central Texas and supports community organizations.

facts.fact: Houston’s Vietnamese and Chinese communities celebrate Lunar New Year with dragon dances and fireworks.
facts.background: These celebrations highlight the cultural diversity of Houston and its large Asian American population.

facts.fact: The town of Lockhart is known as the 'Barbecue Capital of Texas.'
facts.background: Multiple long-standing barbecue joints around the courthouse square attract visitors seeking smoked brisket and sausage.
facts.source: Texas barbecue rankings and travel guides

facts.fact: The 'Texas bug' tradition refers to decorative metal cutouts of the state placed on houses and fences.
facts.background: These Texas-shaped silhouettes, often in black or rusted metal, are a subtle way to show state pride in home decor.

facts.fact: The town of Jefferson in East Texas promotes itself as a historic riverport and 'Bed & Breakfast Capital of Texas.'
facts.background: Its preserved buildings, antique shops, and river tours create a charming, old-fashioned getaway.

facts.fact: Drive-in movie theaters still operate in several Texas towns, offering nostalgic outdoor film nights.
facts.background: Families and friends park their cars, tune radios to movie audio, and enjoy films under the night sky.

facts.fact: The World’s Largest Cowboy Hat is displayed at an outlet mall in northern Texas.
facts.background: Oversized hats, boots, and belt buckles around the state playfully exaggerate cowboy fashion.

facts.fact: Many Texas dance halls, especially in the Hill Country, still host live country, Tejano, and polka music on weekends.
facts.background: Wooden floors, long tables, and neon signs create an atmosphere where multiple generations gather to dance.

facts.fact: The Chicken Ranch in La Grange inspired a Broadway musical and film called 'The Best Little Whorehouse in Texas.'
facts.background: Though the real establishment closed decades ago, its story became part of Texas pop culture lore.

facts.fact: In many Texas towns, local festivals feature jalapeño-eating contests and salsa competitions.
facts.background: Spicy foods reflect the influence of Mexican and Tex-Mex cuisine and the state’s love of bold flavors.

facts.fact: The town of Shiner is home to a famous Texas brewery that began in the early 1900s.
facts.background: The brewery’s signature beers became popular across Texas, and tours invite visitors to learn about its history and process.

facts.fact: Texas roadside stands often sell seasonal fruits like peaches, watermelons, and cantaloupes directly from local growers.
facts.background: Buying fresh produce at highway stands is a summertime tradition for many Texans on road trips.

facts.fact: The Big Tex Choice Awards at the State Fair of Texas honor the most creative new fair foods each year.
facts.background: Winning entries often include deep-fried or over-the-top combinations that grab headlines and long lines.
facts.source: State Fair of Texas press releases

facts.fact: Urban legend claims there are alligators living in some Texas golf course water hazards and drainage canals.
facts.background: While alligators do live in East and coastal Texas wetlands, stories about them on fairways mix wildlife facts with tall tales.
facts.source: Wildlife agency notes and local anecdotes

facts.fact: The town of Grapevine near Dallas–Fort Worth Airport brands itself as the 'Christmas Capital of Texas.'
facts.background: Holiday decorations, events, and vintage train rides draw families for seasonal celebrations.

facts.fact: Some Texas high school marching bands are as large as small colleges and perform elaborate halftime shows.
facts.background: Music programs in football-obsessed towns invest heavily in uniforms, instruments, and travel for competitions.

facts.fact: In Terrell, Texas, you can find a museum dedicated to British Royal Air Force pilots who trained there during World War II.
facts.background: The No. 1 British Flying Training School Museum preserves the story of transatlantic cooperation and the town’s unusual wartime connection.

facts.fact: Texas diners and roadside cafes often serve chicken-fried steak as a comfort food staple.
facts.background: The dish typically features battered, fried beef steak with cream gravy, mashed potatoes, and vegetables.

facts.fact: Kolaches, a pastry of Czech origin, have become a common breakfast item at Texas bakeries and gas stations.
facts.background: Sweet versions with fruit or cream cheese and savory versions with sausage and cheese reflect the blending of Czech and Texan tastes.

facts.fact: The town of Ennis holds an annual bluebonnet trail and festival to celebrate the state flower.
facts.background: Visitors drive through designated routes to see fields of bluebonnets and enjoy live music, crafts, and local foods.

facts.fact: Texas license plates and bumper stickers frequently display slogans like 'Native Texan' or 'I Got Here as Fast as I Could.'
facts.background: These humor-filled phrases reflect pride in being from Texas—or in choosing to move there.

facts.fact: Many Texas homes and businesses display metal stars as a decorative symbol of the Lone Star State.
facts.background: These five-pointed stars appear on porches, fences, and walls, echoing the single star on the state flag.

facts.fact: The city of Beaumont has a giant working fire hydrant painted with Dalmatian spots outside a fire museum.
facts.background: The oversize hydrant is a playful landmark that draws families and photography enthusiasts.

facts.fact: In some Texas towns, rodeo week is treated almost like a local holiday.
facts.background: Schools may host themed days, businesses decorate with Western motifs, and parades kick off days of roping and riding events.

facts.fact: Texans often debate whose hometown has the best tacos, barbecue, or chili.
facts.background: Friendly arguments over food preferences and regional styles are a common part of Texas social life.

facts.fact: Urban legends in Texas include ghost stories about courthouses, hotels, and railroad tracks.
facts.background: Haunted tours in cities like San Antonio, Galveston, and Austin share tales of restless spirits and mysterious events.

facts.fact: The small West Texas town of Marfa has become a hub for minimalist and contemporary art.
facts.background: Artist Donald Judd established studios and installations there, turning an isolated ranching town into an international art destination.

facts.fact: The 'Prada Marfa' installation is a fake luxury boutique standing alone on a highway outside Marfa.
facts.background: The art piece looks like a high-end store but is actually a sealed sculpture filled with real shoes and bags.

facts.fact: Texas boasts numerous roadside BBQ pits where the smoke rising from the pit is often the best advertisement.
facts.background: Many beloved barbecue spots have simple signs, long lines, and a focus on meat quality rather than fancy decor.

facts.fact: Trail rides into Houston before the rodeo involve riders traveling for days on horseback and wagons.
facts.background: These rides honor the state’s ranching and trail-driving heritage, converging on Houston in time for the livestock show and rodeo.

facts.fact: The town of Nacogdoches claims to be the oldest town in Texas, with settlement roots going back centuries.
facts.background: Local festivals, brick streets, and historical sites celebrate its long history in East Texas.

facts.fact: The World Championship Goat Cook-Off in Brady, Texas, celebrates goat meat cuisine with a side of music and fun.
facts.background: Teams compete to cook the best goat dishes, while visitors sample food and enjoy small-town hospitality.

facts.fact: Texas chili cook-offs often insist that 'real Texas chili' should not contain beans.
facts.background: Purists argue that traditional chili is made with meat and spices only, leaving beans for other dishes.

facts.fact: In many Texas communities, church potlucks can feature dishes that blend Southern, Mexican, and Czech influences.
facts.background: Tables may include enchiladas, casseroles, kolaches, and pies side by side.

facts.fact: The word 'y’all' is widely used in Texas as a plural form of 'you.'
facts.background: The term is common in speech across the state and is considered shorter and friendlier than saying 'you all.'

facts.fact: Texas college towns like Austin, Denton, and San Marcos have vibrant local music scenes fueled by student audiences.
facts.background: Bars, coffee shops, and small venues host live bands most nights of the week.

facts.fact: Some Texas cemeteries have elaborate above-ground tombs and folk art gravestones.
facts.background: Graves may incorporate religious symbols, portraits, or symbolic objects reflecting the life of the person buried there.

facts.fact: The town of Salado is known for art galleries, bed-and-breakfasts, and craft shops along a historic main street.
facts.background: Artists and craftspeople sell pottery, jewelry, and textile work in a village-like setting.

facts.fact: Texas has multiple places named after other world cities, including Paris, Athens, Palestine, and Florence.
facts.background: Early settlers sometimes named new towns after distant places, creating a map full of international references.

facts.fact: In some Texas neighborhoods, it’s common to see backyard smokers or pits for weekend barbecue.
facts.background: Smoking brisket or ribs over low heat for many hours is a social activity as much as a cooking method.

facts.fact: The small town of Bowie celebrates 'Jim Bowie Days' with rodeos and frontier-themed events.
facts.background: The festival honors the Alamo hero and local Western traditions.

facts.fact: Some Texas grocery stores have entire aisles devoted to hot sauce varieties.
facts.background: From mild salsas to extreme heat, Texans enjoy a wide range of spicy condiments.

facts.fact: In parts of Texas, drive-thru beverage barns allow customers to buy sodas, snacks, and other drinks without leaving their vehicles.
facts.background: These convenient shops reflect a car-centered lifestyle and warm climate.

facts.fact: The city of Denton hosts music festivals that highlight indie bands, jazz, and experimental sounds.
facts.background: With two universities and strong music departments, Denton nurtures many emerging artists.

facts.fact: The concept of 'float the river' refers to tubing down Hill Country rivers as a casual all-day activity.
facts.background: Groups of friends tie tubes together, bring coolers, and spend hours drifting with the current.

facts.fact: Some Texas travelers collect photos of themselves at each state border sign as they enter and leave Texas.
facts.background: Because Texas is so large, border crossings can be memorable milestones on long road trips.

facts.fact: The Houston area has popular crawfish boils during spring, influenced by neighboring Louisiana traditions.
facts.background: Spiced crawfish, corn, potatoes, and sausage are served at backyard gatherings and restaurants.
facts.source: Food culture and Gulf Coast culinary profiles

facts.fact: Texans sometimes refer to leaving the state as 'going out of country,' half joking about Texas’s outsized identity.
facts.background: The expression reflects the idea that Texas feels distinct enough to be almost its own country.

facts.fact: Texas has multiple 'world’s largest' items, including boots, pecans, and belt buckles, scattered across different towns.
facts.background: These oversized objects serve as quirky local landmarks and photo opportunities.

facts.fact: At many Texas festivals, you’ll see funnel cakes, turkey legs, corn dogs, and lemonade stands lining the midway.
facts.background: Fair foods are part of the sensory experience of rides, games, and live music.

facts.fact: San Antonio’s River Walk is often decorated with colorful lights and papel picado banners during holidays and festivals.
facts.background: Restaurants and bars along the river add to the festive atmosphere with outdoor seating and live music.

facts.fact: Texas convenience stores often sell breakfast tacos in the morning alongside coffee.
facts.background: Eggs, potatoes, chorizo, bacon, and cheese wrapped in tortillas make quick, portable meals for commuters.

facts.fact: Some Texans consider 'Dr Pepper and peanuts' or 'coke and peanuts' a nostalgic snack, pouring peanuts directly into the bottle or cup.
facts.background: The salty-sweet combination dates back decades and is fondly remembered as a simple, old-fashioned treat.

facts.fact: Texas has many local festivals dedicated to specific foods, including peaches, chilies, crawfish, onions, and more.
facts.background: These festivals showcase local crops and recipes while providing music, vendors, and family activities.

facts.fact: From giant roadside statues to unique small-town festivals, Texas embraces a big, bold, and sometimes quirky sense of fun.
facts.background: These playful traditions add color to everyday life and give travelers endless stories and photo ops across the state.

facts.fact: The Texas Legislature meets in regular session only once every two years.
facts.background: The Texas Legislature convenes in odd-numbered years for 140 days, making it one of the few large states with a biennial legislative session.

facts.fact: Texas has a plural executive branch, meaning many statewide officials are elected separately.
facts.background: Offices such as governor, lieutenant governor, attorney general, comptroller, and agriculture commissioner are independently elected, limiting the governor’s direct control.

facts.fact: The lieutenant governor of Texas is considered one of the most powerful state officials.
facts.background: The lieutenant governor presides over the Senate, appoints committee chairs, and has significant influence on which bills move forward.

facts.fact: Texas has 254 counties, more than any other U.S. state.
facts.background: County governments provide local services such as law enforcement, courts, road maintenance, and record-keeping across the vast state.

facts.fact: County judges in Texas often serve as both administrative leaders and heads of county emergency management.
facts.background: Despite the title 'judge,' many county judges focus primarily on executive functions, especially in larger counties.

facts.fact: Texas uses a system of property taxes collected primarily by local governments to fund schools and services.
facts.background: Because Texas has no state income tax, property taxes and sales taxes make up major portions of public revenue.

facts.fact: The Permanent School Fund helps support Texas public education using revenues from state lands and investments.
facts.background: Created in the 19th century, the fund invests oil, gas, and land proceeds for long-term support of school districts.

facts.fact: The Texas Railroad Commission regulates oil and gas development in the state.
facts.background: Originally in charge of railroads, the agency’s primary modern duties involve energy regulation and pipeline oversight.

facts.fact: The Texas Education Agency oversees public primary and secondary education.
facts.background: TEA is responsible for curriculum standards, accountability ratings, testing, and support for thousands of public schools.

facts.fact: Texas maintains its own electric grid managed largely by the Electric Reliability Council of Texas, or ERCOT.
facts.background: Most of the state is on a stand-alone grid not fully synchronized with other U.S. regions, giving Texas more regulatory autonomy over electricity.

facts.fact: Texas is a national leader in both oil and natural gas production.
facts.background: Fields in the Permian Basin, Eagle Ford Shale, and other regions have made Texas central to U.S. energy supplies for decades.

facts.fact: Texas is one of the top wind energy-producing state in the United States.
facts.background: Wind farms across West Texas and the Panhandle contribute thousands of megawatts of electricity to the grid.

facts.fact: Houston is known as the 'Energy Capital of the World.'
facts.background: The city’s economy is heavily tied to the oil, gas, petrochemical, and energy services industries, and it hosts many corporate headquarters.

facts.fact: The Port of Houston is consistently one of the busiest ports in the United States by tonnage.
facts.background: Located along the Houston Ship Channel, the port handles crude oil, refined products, chemicals, and containerized cargo from around the world.

facts.fact: The Dallas–Fort Worth area is a major center for telecommunications, technology, and finance.
facts.background: Companies in the Metroplex work in fields ranging from banking and insurance to aviation, software, and logistics.

facts.fact: San Antonio’s economy includes military bases, cybersecurity, health care, and tourism.
facts.background: Large military installations and growing tech and medical sectors diversify the city’s economic base beyond the River Walk and Alamo tourism.

facts.fact: The Texas Medical Center in Houston is the largest medical complex in the world.
facts.background: It includes dozens of hospitals, research institutions, and specialty centers focused on everything from cancer care to heart surgery.

facts.fact: NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston has managed U.S. human spaceflight missions since the 1960s.
facts.background: Flight control rooms, astronaut training facilities, and research labs in Houston have supported missions from Gemini and Apollo to the ISS.

facts.fact: Neil Armstrong's transmission 'Houston, Tranquility Base here. The Eagle has landed' connected the Moon to NASA's Mission Control in Houston.
facts.background: During the Apollo 11 landing on July 20, 1969, Armstrong confirmed the first successful lunar landing by radioing Houston — cementing the city's place in space history.

facts.fact: Texas is home to multiple Tier One research universities.
facts.background: Universities such as UT Austin, Texas A&M, and others conduct cutting-edge research in engineering, medicine, energy, and computing.

facts.fact: The Texas Advanced Computing Center at UT Austin operates some of the world’s powerful academic supercomputers.
facts.background: These systems support research in climate modeling, astrophysics, biomedical science, and many other fields.

facts.fact: Texas has a large network of community colleges providing workforce training and academic transfer programs.
facts.background: Community colleges help students prepare for technical careers and offer lower-cost pathways to four-year degrees.

facts.fact: The Texas Constitution requires the state to support a system of free public schools.
facts.background: Court cases and legislation have repeatedly revisited how school funding is raised and distributed among districts.

facts.fact: Texas public schools serve over five million students each year.
facts.background: The state’s student population is one of the largest in the nation, with diverse languages, cultures, and educational needs.

facts.fact: The Texas economy ranks among the largest of any U.S. state and rivals many countries.
facts.background: Driven by energy, technology, agriculture, manufacturing, and trade, Texas’s gross state product is comparable to that of major nations.

facts.fact: Texas has no state income tax on individuals.
facts.background: Instead, the state relies heavily on sales taxes, property taxes, and various fees to fund government services.

facts.fact: The Texas Department of Transportation, or TxDOT, manages one of the largest highway systems in the country.
facts.background: Thousands of miles of interstate, U.S., and state highways connect Texas cities, small towns, and rural areas.

facts.fact: Major interstate highways such as I-10, I-35, and I-45 cross Texas and connect to other states.
facts.background: These roads support freight movement, commuting, and tourism across a geographically large state.

facts.fact: Texas is a major hub for air travel, with several large international airports.
facts.background: Dallas–Fort Worth International Airport and Houston’s Bush Intercontinental Airport are among the busiest in the nation.

facts.fact: The state’s agriculture includes cattle, cotton, sorghum, poultry, and many other products.
facts.background: From Panhandle feedlots to Rio Grande Valley produce farms, agriculture remains a major contributor to the Texas economy.

facts.fact: Texas is a leading exporter of goods such as petroleum products, chemicals, electronics, and machinery.
facts.background: Ports, railroads, and highways help Texas companies ship products to markets around the world.

facts.fact: The Texas water supply relies on a combination of rivers, reservoirs, aquifers, and conservation efforts.
facts.background: State water plans look decades ahead to account for population growth, drought cycles, and competing demands.
facts.source: Texas Water Development Board planning documents

facts.fact: The Edwards Aquifer is a critical water source for Central Texas communities like San Antonio.
facts.background: This karst aquifer stores water in underground limestone formations and feeds springs and rivers in the region.

facts.fact: Texas participates in regional air quality and environmental programs to manage ozone and pollution.
facts.background: Urban areas such as Houston and Dallas–Fort Worth monitor emissions from vehicles and industries to meet federal standards.
facts.source: Texas Commission on Environmental Quality resources

facts.fact: The Texas State Library and Archives Commission preserves legislative documents, maps, and historical records.
facts.background: Researchers use these archives to study topics ranging from early colonization to modern public policy.

facts.fact: Texas has a two-tier appellate court system for criminal and civil cases.
facts.background: The Texas Supreme Court handles civil matters, while the Court of Criminal Appeals is the highest court for criminal cases.

facts.fact: Texas cities often use council–manager or mayor–council forms of local government.
facts.background: Charter cities can structure powers among elected councils, mayors, and professional city managers to meet local needs.

facts.fact: Texas is a major center for trucking and logistics due to its size and location.
facts.background: Highways and distribution hubs connect cross-country freight routes from ports and border crossings.

facts.fact: Multiple North American Free Trade Agreement–era trade corridors pass through Texas.
facts.background: Highways and rail lines connect Mexican manufacturing centers with U.S. and Canadian markets through Texas ports of entry.

facts.fact: Texas has several major land ports of entry with Mexico, including Laredo, El Paso, and Brownsville.
facts.background: These border crossings handle large volumes of commercial trucks and passenger vehicles every day.
facts.source: U.S. customs and border crossing statistics

facts.fact: Laredo is one of the busiest inland ports in the United States for international trade.
facts.background: Rail and truck traffic carrying automotive parts, electronics, and other goods move through Laredo’s bridges and customs facilities.

facts.fact: Texas law enforcement includes state-level agencies such as the Texas Department of Public Safety.
facts.background: DPS oversees the Texas Highway Patrol, state criminal investigations, and driver licensing services.

facts.fact: The state maintains guard units as part of the Texas Military Department, including the Texas Army National Guard and Texas Air National Guard.
facts.background: These units support federal missions overseas and respond to state emergencies such as hurricanes and floods.

facts.fact: Science and technology sectors in Texas include aerospace, biotechnology, semiconductors, and software development.
facts.background: Cities like Austin, Dallas, and Houston have attracted startups and established firms in various tech industries.

facts.fact: Austin’s tech growth has earned it nicknames like 'Silicon Hills.'
facts.background: Software, chip design, and hardware companies operate alongside a vibrant startup scene and university research.
facts.source: Austin economic development and tech reports

facts.fact: Texas universities participate in extensive agricultural and engineering extension programs.
facts.background: Extension services share research-based information with farmers, ranchers, businesses, and communities across the state.
facts.source: Texas A&M AgriLife and extension program descriptions

facts.fact: Many Texas school districts are governed by locally elected school boards.
facts.background: School boards make decisions about budgets, policies, and superintendents, reflecting community priorities in public education.

facts.fact: The state’s transportation network includes freight rail lines that move energy, chemicals, and agricultural goods.
facts.background: Major railroads cross Texas, linking ports and inland hubs with the rest of the country.

facts.fact: Texas faces recurring challenges balancing water needs for cities, agriculture, industry, and ecosystems.
facts.background: Droughts and population growth spark debates about new reservoirs, conservation measures, and inter-basin transfers.

facts.fact: The Texas Constitution is unusually long and detailed compared to many other state constitutions.
facts.background: Its length comes from numerous amendments and the practice of specifying policies in the constitution rather than general law.

facts.fact: Public universities in Texas are organized into multiple systems, such as the UT System and Texas A&M System.
facts.background: Each system oversees several campuses with their own missions, degrees, and research specialties.

facts.fact: The state has invested in research and technology commercialization through various grant programs.
facts.background: These programs aim to turn university and private-sector innovations into new companies and jobs.
facts.source: State innovation and research grant materials

facts.fact: Texas plays a major role in U.S. spaceflight beyond NASA, with growing commercial space companies operating in the state.
facts.background: Test facilities, launch sites, and manufacturing plants in Texas contribute to the emerging commercial space industry.
facts.source: Commercial space industry news and reports

facts.fact: The Texas economy benefits from its central time zone and location between coasts.
facts.background: Businesses can more easily coordinate with both East and West Coast partners and international markets in Europe and the Americas.

facts.fact: Texas public higher education includes specialized institutions for health sciences, law, and fine arts.
facts.background: Medical schools, law schools, and arts academies across the state train professionals for specialized careers.

facts.fact: Residents in many Texas cities vote on bond elections to fund schools, roads, and public facilities.
facts.background: Bond propositions allow local governments to borrow money for capital projects with voter approval.

facts.fact: Texas is a major producer of wind turbine components and related manufacturing.
facts.background: Factories in the state build blades, towers, and other parts that support the renewable energy supply chain.

facts.fact: The state’s demographic growth has made Texas a key focus during each U.S. Census.
facts.background: Population gains affect congressional representation, federal funding, and planning for roads, schools, and hospitals.

facts.fact: Texas has large technology corridors in Austin, Dallas–Fort Worth, Houston, and San Antonio.
facts.background: These corridors include clusters of tech companies, incubators, and research parks that encourage innovation.

facts.fact: Many Texas students participate in career and technical education programs in high school.
facts.background: CTE pathways prepare students for jobs in fields like health care, welding, information technology, and culinary arts.

facts.fact: The Texas Legislature uses a committee system to review and amend proposed bills.
facts.background: Committees hold hearings, gather testimony, and decide whether bills advance to the full House or Senate for debate.

facts.fact: Texas has an independent ethics commission that oversees campaign finance and certain public conduct rules.
facts.background: The commission administers reporting requirements and enforces state ethics laws for officeholders and candidates.

facts.fact: The state’s criminal justice system includes local jails, state prisons, and community supervision programs.
facts.background: Different agencies and levels of government manage these institutions, which house inmates and provide probation and parole supervision.

facts.fact: Texas has historically been one of the top states for new business formation.
facts.background: A large population, diverse industries, and a reputation for low taxes and flexible regulation attract entrepreneurs.

facts.fact: The state’s airports and highways make Texas a major center for package delivery and e-commerce logistics.
facts.background: Sorting centers and distribution hubs for multiple carriers are located in and around Texas metro areas.

facts.fact: Texas libraries, museums, and science centers offer extensive public education programs.
facts.background: Institutions in cities and small towns provide exhibits, lectures, and hands-on activities for learners of all ages.

facts.fact: The Texas Higher Education Coordinating Board helps plan and coordinate public colleges and universities.
facts.background: The board sets statewide goals for access, affordability, and completion, and reviews new degree programs.

facts.fact: Many Texas cities operate public transit systems including buses, light rail, or commuter rail.
facts.background: Dallas, Houston, Austin, San Antonio, and others provide transit options to reduce congestion and expand mobility.

facts.fact: Texas has significant petrochemical complexes located along the Gulf Coast.
facts.background: Refineries and chemical plants produce fuels, plastics, fertilizers, and other industrial products.

facts.fact: Science education in Texas emphasizes both state standards and participation in national competitions.
facts.background: Students compete in science fairs, robotics contests, and STEM Olympiads with support from schools and community organizations.

facts.fact: Texas hosts multiple military bases that support Army, Air Force, and joint operations.
facts.background: Installations such as Fort Cavazos (formerly Fort Hood), Joint Base San Antonio, and others contribute billions to local economies.

facts.fact: The state’s long coastline requires extensive hurricane preparedness and evacuation planning.
facts.background: Emergency managers coordinate routes, shelters, and communication strategies for storms that threaten coastal communities.

facts.fact: Texas uses a sales tax that is collected by the state and supplemented by local option sales taxes.
facts.background: Cities, counties, and special districts may add local sales taxes up to a capped combined rate.

facts.fact: The state’s economic development efforts seek to attract companies through incentives, workforce training, and infrastructure.
facts.background: Programs focus on sectors like advanced manufacturing, biotech, logistics, and energy innovation.

facts.fact: Texas participates in regional and national grids for gas and oil pipelines.
facts.background: Pipelines transport crude oil, refined products, and natural gas from Texas fields to other states and export terminals.

facts.fact: The state’s higher education goals include increasing the share of adults with postsecondary credentials.
facts.background: Strategic plans emphasize workforce readiness and competitiveness in a knowledge-based economy.

facts.fact: Texas has multiple regional councils of governments that coordinate planning across city and county lines.
facts.background: These councils address issues like transportation, environmental quality, and emergency services that cross jurisdictional boundaries.

facts.fact: Texas schools use standardized tests aligned to the State of Texas Assessments of Academic Readiness, or STAAR.
facts.background: STAAR exams measure student performance against state curriculum standards in core subjects.

facts.fact: The state’s economy benefits from both domestic migration and international immigration.
facts.background: New residents bring skills, labor, and entrepreneurial energy to cities and towns across Texas.

facts.fact: Texas promotes science and math education through initiatives like STEM academies and magnet schools.
facts.background: Specialized schools and programs provide rigorous coursework and hands-on projects to prepare students for technical careers.
facts.source: District and state STEM program descriptions

facts.fact: Texas’s natural gas-fueled power plants supply a significant share of the state’s electricity.
facts.background: Abundant local gas resources make it a common fuel for electricity generation, complementing wind and solar.

facts.fact: The state has been expanding solar power, especially in West and Central Texas.
facts.background: High solar potential and falling panel costs have led to large-scale photovoltaic farm development.

facts.fact: Texas courts handle a large volume of civil and criminal cases each year at the local, county, and state levels.
facts.background: Justices of the peace, county courts, district courts, and appellate courts share responsibility for resolving legal disputes.

facts.fact: Public–private partnerships in Texas help finance toll roads, transit projects, and other infrastructure.
facts.background: These arrangements allow private firms to invest in public projects in exchange for long-term revenues or leases.

facts.fact: Texas has implemented statewide emergency alerts for severe weather, Amber Alerts, and other public safety issues.
facts.background: Television, radio, highway signs, and mobile phones are used to broadcast urgent information quickly.
facts.source: Public safety and emergency alert system overviews

facts.fact: Civics education in Texas includes instruction about the U.S. and Texas constitutions, rights, and responsibilities.
facts.background: Courses in U.S. history, government, and economics teach students about democratic processes and public policy.

facts.fact: The state’s diverse economy means Texas is active in industries from ranching and farming to high tech and advanced manufacturing.
facts.background: This diversity can help cushion regional downturns, even though individual sectors still face boom-and-bust cycles.

facts.fact: Texas has invested in broadband expansion to reach more rural and underserved communities.
facts.background: Improved internet access supports education, telehealth, business, and civic participation in remote areas.

facts.fact: Major Texas cities collaborate with universities and private industry to create innovation districts and research parks.
facts.background: These districts concentrate labs, startups, and investors to encourage collaboration and commercialization of new ideas.

facts.fact: Texas uses regional planning organizations to coordinate long-range transportation plans.
facts.background: Metropolitan planning organizations work with cities, counties, and TxDOT to prioritize highway and transit projects.

facts.fact: The state’s large manufacturing sector includes petrochemicals, electronics, vehicles, and aerospace components.
facts.background: Factories in different regions specialize in different products, contributing to exports and domestic supply chains.

facts.fact: Texas’s size and population mean it sends one of the largest delegations to the U.S. House of Representatives.
facts.background: Every ten years, redistricting adjusts congressional districts based on new Census data.

facts.fact: Environmental science research in Texas examines coastal erosion, hurricanes, water quality, and air pollution.
facts.background: Universities and agencies monitor environmental changes to inform resilience and conservation strategies.

facts.fact: Texas’s tourism industry includes historical sites, state parks, coastal beaches, and major sports and music events.
facts.background: Visitors spend billions each year on lodging, food, entertainment, and transportation throughout the state.
facts.source: State tourism and economic impact reports

facts.fact: The state’s public pension systems support retired teachers, state employees, and local government workers.
facts.background: Managing these funds involves long-term investment strategies and periodic legislative adjustments.

facts.fact: Texas is a major hub for data centers and cloud computing facilities.
facts.background: Reliable power, fiber connectivity, and central location make Texas attractive for companies storing and processing digital data.

facts.fact: Public health agencies in Texas monitor disease outbreaks, vaccinations, and emergency medical response.
facts.background: The state health department works with local health authorities to protect community health across urban and rural areas.
facts.source: Texas Department of State Health Services information

facts.fact: Texas’s long-term transportation goals include improving safety, reducing congestion, and maintaining key freight corridors.
facts.background: Strategic plans balance the needs of drivers, transit riders, freight carriers, and pedestrians.

facts.fact: Science and engineering fairs in Texas encourage students to pursue research projects and STEM careers.
facts.background: Local and state-level competitions allow students to present experiments, prototypes, and innovations to judges and peers.

facts.fact: Texas’s blend of strong local governments, a large and diverse economy, and major research institutions makes it a key player in national policy and innovation.
facts.background: From energy and space exploration to education and infrastructure, decisions made in Texas reverberate far beyond its borders.
facts.source: Comprehensive Texas policy and economic overviews

facts.fact: Texas is home to two current NFL franchises: the Dallas Cowboys and the Houston Texans.
facts.background: The Dallas Cowboys play in the NFC East and the Houston Texans play in the AFC South, giving Texas a pro football presence in both NFL conferences.

facts.fact: The Dallas Cowboys have won five Super Bowl championships.
facts.background: Dallas captured Super Bowls VI, XII, XXVII, XXVIII, and XXX, becoming one of the NFL’s most decorated franchises.

facts.fact: The Dallas Cowboys have appeared in eight Super Bowls.
facts.background: Their eight Super Bowl appearances tie the Cowboys with several other teams for the second-most appearances in NFL history.

facts.fact: From 1966 to 1985, the Dallas Cowboys posted 20 straight winning seasons.
facts.background: That two-decade run of winning records under coach Tom Landry is one of the longest sustained stretches of success in NFL history.

facts.fact: The Dallas Cowboys set the record for the most-watched NFL regular-season game with a Thanksgiving win over the Kansas City Chiefs.
facts.background: Their 31–28 Thanksgiving Day victory over Kansas City drew about 57.2 million viewers, the highest regular-season TV audience in league history.

facts.fact: AT&T Stadium in Arlington, home of the Cowboys, seats about 80,000 fans and can expand to over 100,000 with standing room.
facts.background: Opened in 2009, the retractable-roof stadium has one of the largest capacities in the NFL and regularly hosts major football events in addition to Cowboys games.

facts.fact: AT&T Stadium features a massive center-hung video board that stretches roughly 160 feet.
facts.background: The huge high-definition screen was one of the largest of its kind when the stadium opened and is suspended about 90 feet above the field.

facts.fact: The Houston Texans were granted as an NFL expansion franchise in 1999 and began play in 2002.
facts.background: They became the NFL’s 32nd franchise and returned pro football to Houston after the Oilers moved to Tennessee in the 1990s.

facts.fact: The Houston Texans are the second NFL franchise in Houston, following the Houston Oilers.
facts.background: The Oilers played in Houston from 1960 to 1996 before relocating and eventually becoming the Tennessee Titans, leaving a gap later filled by the Texans.

facts.fact: The Houston Texans have won multiple AFC South division titles but have never reached a Super Bowl.
facts.background: Houston has claimed several AFC South crowns and made multiple playoff appearances but has yet to advance to a conference championship game or the Super Bowl.

facts.fact: The 1939 Texas A&M Aggies football team finished 11–0 and was recognized as national champion.
facts.background: Behind fullback John Kimbrough, the Aggies went undefeated, won the Southwest Conference, and edged Tulane in the Sugar Bowl to secure a consensus national title.

facts.fact: Texas A&M’s 1939 national championship team outscored opponents 212–31.
facts.background: The Aggies shut out more than half of their opponents and allowed less than two yards per play on defense, making it one of the stingiest units of its era.

facts.fact: The Texas Longhorns claim four modern-era football national championships.
facts.background: Texas recognizes national titles in 1963, 1969, 1970, and 2005, with the 2005 championship sealed in a classic Rose Bowl win over USC.

facts.fact: The Texas Longhorns rank among the all-time winningest college football programs and sit in the top five for total victories.
facts.background: Texas has accumulated hundreds of wins since the late 1800s and consistently appears near the top of NCAA all-time win lists.

facts.fact: The Cotton Bowl Classic has been played in the Dallas–Fort Worth area since 1937.
facts.background: Originally held at the Cotton Bowl stadium in Dallas, the bowl moved to AT&T Stadium in Arlington in 2010 and is now part of the College Football Playoff rotation.

facts.fact: The Red River Rivalry between Texas and Oklahoma is traditionally played at the Cotton Bowl in Dallas during the State Fair of Texas.
facts.background: The neutral-site game splits the stadium between burnt orange and crimson and is one of college football’s most famous rivalries.

facts.fact: Texas high school football participation has grown to over 170,000 players in 11-man football alone.
facts.background: NFHS data shows that 11-player football participation in Texas rose from about 164,554 players in 2013–14 to around 171,246 in 2023–24.

facts.fact: Texas routinely leads the nation in total high school sports participation.
facts.background: Recent NFHS surveys list Texas at more than 850,000 combined boys and girls sports participants, the highest total of any state.

facts.fact: Six-man football is a major part of small-school life in rural West and Central Texas.
facts.background: Tiny high schools that can’t field full 11-man rosters play six-man football under UIL-sanctioned divisions, producing wide-open, high-scoring games under the Friday night lights.

facts.fact: The University Interscholastic League (UIL) governs public school football in Texas from Class 1A through Class 6A.
facts.background: UIL organizes regular seasons and playoffs for hundreds of Texas schools, culminating in state championship games across multiple classifications.

facts.fact: Texas high school football state championships are held at AT&T Stadium each December.
facts.background: Teams from the smallest six-man schools to the largest 6A programs converge on Arlington to play for state titles on the same field as the Dallas Cowboys.

facts.fact: The book "Friday Night Lights" is based on a Texas high school football team in Odessa.
facts.background: H. G. Bissinger’s 1990 book follows the 1988 Permian High School Panthers, capturing the town’s intense football culture and inspiring both a movie and TV series.
facts.source: Friday Night Lights by H. G. Bissinger

facts.fact: The phrase "Friday Night Lights" has become shorthand for Texas high school football culture.
facts.background: Because of the book, film, and TV series, the term now evokes small-town Texas stadiums lit up on autumn Friday nights across the state.

facts.fact: Some Texas high school football stadiums are larger than many small-college venues.
facts.background: Several high school stadiums in Texas seat tens of thousands of fans and feature amenities like video boards, luxury boxes, and artificial turf.

facts.fact: Texas consistently ranks among the top states for producing NFL players.
facts.background: Annual NFL player birthplace and high school data regularly list Texas alongside California and Florida as one of the biggest pipelines of professional football talent.

facts.fact: Hall of Fame running back Earl Campbell starred for the Texas Longhorns before playing for the Houston Oilers.
facts.background: The "Tyler Rose" won the Heisman Trophy at Texas in 1977 and became an NFL MVP and rushing champion with Houston.

facts.fact: Ricky Williams won the Heisman Trophy as a Texas Longhorn in 1998.
facts.background: Williams broke the NCAA career rushing record at the time and is remembered as one of the greatest running backs in college football history.

facts.fact: The rivalry between the Texas Longhorns and Texas A&M Aggies is known as the Lone Star Showdown.
facts.background: First played in 1894, the football series became a Thanksgiving tradition for decades and has recently resumed in the expanded SEC era.
facts.source: University of Texas and Texas A&M athletics

facts.fact: The Texas Longhorns’ dramatic 38–37 win over Michigan in the 2005 Rose Bowl is one of the program’s most famous games.
facts.background: Quarterback Vince Young led a late game-winning drive, setting the stage for Texas’ national championship run the following season.

facts.fact: The 2005 Texas Longhorns won the national championship by defeating USC in the Rose Bowl.
facts.background: In one of the greatest title games ever, Vince Young scored the game-winning touchdown in the final seconds to secure Texas’ 2005 national crown.

facts.fact: Darrell K Royal–Texas Memorial Stadium in Austin ranks among the largest college football stadiums in the country.
facts.background: Capacity has been expanded over the years to hold more than 100,000 fans for Texas home games.

facts.fact: Kyle Field at Texas A&M is one of the loudest and largest stadiums in college football.
facts.background: With over 100,000 seats and a famously coordinated 12th Man student section, Kyle Field is known for its intimidating game-day atmosphere.

facts.fact: The Goodyear Cotton Bowl Classic is one of the New Year’s Six bowls used in the College Football Playoff.
facts.background: When designated as a semifinal, the Cotton Bowl sends its winner to play for the national championship, showcasing top teams in Arlington.

facts.fact: Multiple college bowl games are played in Texas each year, including the Texas Bowl and the Alamo Bowl.
facts.background: The Texas Bowl is held at NRG Stadium in Houston, while the Alamo Bowl is played at the Alamodome in San Antonio, bringing major conference matchups to the state.

facts.fact: For many years San Antonio hosted the U.S. Army All-American Bowl for elite high school football players.
facts.background: The all-star game at the Alamodome drew top prospects from across the country and became a major national recruiting showcase.

facts.fact: AT&T Stadium hosted the first College Football Playoff National Championship game in January 2015.
facts.background: Ohio State defeated Oregon in the inaugural CFP title game played in Arlington, cementing the stadium’s status as a premier college football venue.

facts.fact: The Dallas Cowboys consistently rank near the top of the NFL in home attendance.
facts.background: The combination of AT&T Stadium’s huge capacity and the team’s national following helps Dallas draw some of the league’s largest crowds every season.

facts.fact: Texas high school football playoffs feature multiple classifications from 1A through 6A, creating dozens of state title games.
facts.background: Public schools are divided by enrollment size, which means many different champions are crowned each year across 11-man and six-man divisions.

facts.fact: Houston’s NRG Stadium regularly hosts both NFL games and major college football events.
facts.background: In addition to being home to the Texans, NRG Stadium has hosted Super Bowl games, college bowls, and conference championships.

facts.fact: The University of Houston Cougars play many of their biggest football games at TDECU Stadium in Houston.
facts.background: UH has produced numerous NFL players and has appeared in multiple bowl games while representing major conferences like the Southwest Conference, American Athletic, and Big 12.

facts.fact: North Texas, SMU, and other Texas universities add to the state’s dense college football landscape.
facts.background: Schools like SMU in Dallas and North Texas in Denton compete at the FBS level and contribute to the state’s reputation as a year-round football hotbed.

facts.fact: Many Texas high schools treat football game days like community-wide events.
facts.background: In small towns, businesses decorate in school colors and crowds pack local stadiums, making Friday nights a social focal point for the entire community.
facts.source: Sociological studies of Texas high school football

facts.fact: The term "Texas high school football" often appears in national media as a symbol of intense community sports culture.
facts.background: Articles, documentaries, and TV segments regularly use Texas programs as examples when discussing the passion and pressure around prep football.

facts.fact: UIL safety initiatives have emphasized concussion awareness and heat protocols in Texas high school football.
facts.background: Coaches and administrators across Texas have implemented updated practice rules, tackling techniques, and medical guidelines to protect student-athletes.

facts.fact: Texas high school football participation has rebounded strongly after the COVID-19 dip, matching or surpassing pre-2017 levels.
facts.background: Recent NFHS data show that 2023–24 marked one of the largest single-year increases in high school football participation nationwide, with Texas leading the way.

facts.fact: The Houston Astros have won multiple World Series titles, including in 2017 and 2022.
facts.background: Houston’s championships came after decades of chasing a title and included American League pennants in 2017, 2019, 2021, and 2022.

facts.fact: The Texas Rangers won their first World Series championship in 2023.
facts.background: Texas defeated the Arizona Diamondbacks in five games and finished the postseason with a perfect 11–0 record on the road, ending a 63-year title drought for the franchise.

facts.fact: The Texas Rangers are based in Arlington in the Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex.
facts.background: Originally founded as the Washington Senators in 1961, the franchise moved to Texas in the early 1970s and took on the Rangers name.

facts.fact: The University of Texas Longhorns have won multiple NCAA baseball national championships.
facts.background: Texas has claimed six NCAA baseball titles, with championships spread from 1949 to 2005, making it one of college baseball’s historically elite programs.

facts.fact: The Houston Astros played for decades in the Astrodome, nicknamed the "Eighth Wonder of the World."
facts.background: Opened in 1965 in Houston, the Astrodome was the first domed multipurpose stadium and introduced synthetic turf to major professional sports.

facts.fact: The San Antonio Spurs have won five NBA championships.
facts.background: The Spurs captured titles in 1999, 2003, 2005, 2007, and 2014, building one of the most consistent dynasties in NBA history.

facts.fact: For decades, the San Antonio Spurs had one of the best winning percentages in all major North American sports.
facts.background: From the late 1990s through the 2010s, the Spurs consistently won 50 or more games and matched an NBA record with 22 straight playoff appearances.

facts.fact: The Dallas Mavericks won their first NBA championship in 2011.
facts.background: Led by Dirk Nowitzki, the Mavericks defeated the heavily favored Miami Heat in six games in the NBA Finals.

facts.fact: The Houston Rockets won back-to-back NBA championships in 1994 and 1995.
facts.background: With Hakeem Olajuwon dominating in the paint, the Rockets defeated the New York Knicks in 1994 and swept the Orlando Magic in 1995.

facts.fact: The 1995 Houston Rockets are the lowest-seeded team ever to win an NBA championship.
facts.background: Entering the playoffs as a No. 6 seed, Houston upset four 50-plus-win teams and won nine road playoff games to clinch a repeat title.

facts.fact: The Baylor Bears women’s basketball team went 40–0 to win the 2012 NCAA national championship.
facts.background: Their undefeated run capped one of the most dominant seasons in women’s college basketball history.

facts.fact: Houston Dynamo FC won the MLS Cup in both 2006 and 2007.
facts.background: The club captured league titles in its first two seasons after moving to Houston and later added U.S. Open Cup trophies in 2018 and 2023.

facts.fact: FC Dallas is a Major League Soccer club based in Frisco, Texas.
facts.background: Founded as the Dallas Burn in 1995, the team rebranded as FC Dallas in 2004 and plays at Toyota Stadium in the northern Dallas–Fort Worth suburbs.

facts.fact: Austin FC became the first top-tier major professional sports team in the Austin area in 2021.
facts.background: The expansion MLS club played its inaugural season in 2021, opening Q2 Stadium and quickly building a passionate local fan base.

facts.fact: AT&T Stadium in Arlington is scheduled to host matches for the 2026 FIFA World Cup.
facts.background: The massive venue will be one of several U.S. stadiums used during the expanded 48-team World Cup hosted across North America.
facts.source: FIFA and World Cup 2026 venue lists

facts.fact: The Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo is the largest livestock exhibition and rodeo in the world.
facts.background: Held at NRG Stadium, the event combines professional rodeo, concerts, and agricultural exhibitions and drew a record 2.7 million attendees in 2025.

facts.fact: The Houston Rodeo is one of the richest regular-season rodeo events in professional rodeo.
facts.background: Top competitors in events like bull riding, barrel racing, and roping compete for significant prize money in front of huge crowds.

facts.fact: Stock car races in the NASCAR Cup Series have been held at Texas Motor Speedway since 1997.
facts.background: Located in Fort Worth, Texas Motor Speedway hosts major NASCAR events and features a distinctive cowboy-hat trophy for its Cup Series race winner.

facts.fact: Texas Motor Speedway’s Cup Series winner traditionally fires ceremonial six-shooters in Victory Lane.
facts.background: The victor dons a black cowboy hat and celebrates with Texas-themed pageantry, making it one of NASCAR’s most recognizable victory rituals.
facts.source: NASCAR Cup Series at Texas Motor Speedway

facts.fact: Circuit of the Americas (COTA) in Austin has hosted both Formula 1 and NASCAR events.
facts.background: The purpose-built road course has been home to the United States Grand Prix and regularly appears on NASCAR’s Cup Series schedule.
facts.source: Circuit of the Americas and NASCAR

facts.fact: The Houston Rockets, Dallas Mavericks, and San Antonio Spurs give Texas three NBA franchises.
facts.background: Few states have as many NBA teams as Texas, and all three franchises have won at least one NBA championship.

facts.fact: Dallas hosts the annual AT&T Byron Nelson golf tournament on the PGA Tour.
facts.background: Named after golf legend Byron Nelson, the event has long been a staple of the PGA Tour’s Texas swing.

facts.fact: The Charles Schwab Challenge at Colonial Country Club in Fort Worth is one of the PGA Tour’s longest-running events.
facts.background: The Colonial event has been played annually since the 1940s and often draws one of the strongest fields of the year.

facts.fact: Texas has produced numerous Olympic track and field athletes, including sprinters and hurdlers from schools like Baylor and Texas A&M.
facts.background: The state’s warm climate and strong college programs have helped develop elite sprinters and quarter-milers for generations.
facts.source: US Olympic track and field history

facts.fact: The Dallas Stars brought Texas its first Stanley Cup championship in 1999.
facts.background: Playing in Dallas since the early 1990s, the Stars defeated the Buffalo Sabres in the 1999 Stanley Cup Final.

facts.fact: The WNBA’s Dallas Wings play their home games in the Dallas–Fort Worth area.
facts.background: The franchise relocated from Tulsa and represents the growing presence of professional women’s basketball in Texas.

facts.fact: Softball is a major sport in Texas high schools and colleges, with several NCAA Division I programs regularly reaching the postseason.
facts.background: Programs such as Texas, Texas A&M, and Baylor often appear in NCAA regionals and Women’s College World Series brackets.

facts.fact: Volleyball is one of the fastest-growing girls’ sports in Texas high schools.
facts.background: NFHS participation reports show strong growth in volleyball across Texas, fueled by club circuits and strong collegiate programs in the state.

facts.fact: The University of Texas women’s volleyball program has become a perennial national title contender.
facts.background: Texas regularly appears in the Final Four and has won multiple NCAA championships, further raising the sport’s profile in the state.

facts.fact: The Texas Relays in Austin are one of the largest annual track and field meets in the United States.
facts.background: Hosted by the University of Texas, the event draws collegiate, high school, and elite athletes from around the country.

facts.fact: The UIL state track and field championships bring athletes from every corner of Texas to compete in Austin.
facts.background: Multiple classifications compete over several days, showcasing the depth of sprinting, distance running, and field-event talent in the state.

facts.fact: The Houston Marathon is one of the premier road races in the United States.
facts.background: Each January, thousands of runners compete in the marathon and half marathon, which are known for fast, flat courses that often produce qualifying times for major races.

facts.fact: The Austin Marathon brings thousands of runners through downtown Austin every year.
facts.background: The challenging course features hills and scenic views of the Texas capital, drawing runners from across the country and around the world.

facts.fact: Texas is a powerhouse in high school and college baseball, with many players advancing to MLB.
facts.background: Warm weather and a long playing season allow for year-round development, and Texas high school programs are heavily scouted by college and pro teams.

facts.fact: The state of Texas hosts multiple minor league baseball teams across several levels.
facts.background: Clubs in cities like Round Rock, Frisco, Sugar Land, and El Paso serve as affiliates for MLB organizations and provide local fans with affordable pro baseball.

facts.fact: The Houston Cougars men’s basketball program has a long history, including the famed "Phi Slama Jama" teams of the 1980s.
facts.background: Led by stars like Hakeem Olajuwon and Clyde Drexler, those Houston teams reached multiple Final Fours and changed how people viewed above-the-rim college basketball.

facts.fact: The Texas Longhorns men’s basketball team has produced multiple NBA players and frequent NCAA tournament appearances.
facts.background: Alumni include stars like Kevin Durant and LaMarcus Aldridge, who played one season in Austin before moving on to professional careers.

facts.fact: The San Antonio Spurs’ fan base is known for its strong support despite the city being one of the smaller NBA markets.
facts.background: Sell-out crowds packed the arena throughout the team’s championship years, turning San Antonio into a global basketball brand.

facts.fact: Texas universities compete in multiple major conferences, including the SEC, Big 12, American Athletic, Conference USA, and others.
facts.background: Conference realignment has moved Texas schools like Texas and Texas A&M into the SEC, while others remain key members of the Big 12 and additional leagues.

facts.fact: The Texas High School Coaches Association is one of the largest such organizations in the country.
facts.background: It provides education, advocacy, and resources for coaches in football and many other sports across the state.

facts.fact: Texas hosts numerous large youth soccer tournaments that draw teams from across the region and nation.
facts.background: Events in Dallas, Houston, Austin, and San Antonio help develop players who often move on to college and professional soccer careers.

facts.fact: The Dallas Cup is one of the most prestigious international youth soccer tournaments hosted in Texas.
facts.background: Held annually in the Dallas area, it frequently features elite youth teams from Europe, South America, and Mexico as well as top American clubs.

facts.fact: The University of Texas and Texas A&M have historically strong swimming and diving programs.
facts.background: Texas, in particular, has won numerous NCAA men’s swimming and diving championships under legendary coach Eddie Reese.

facts.fact: Rowing and crew have grown in popularity in Texas, especially in cities with major rivers and lakes.
facts.background: Clubs and collegiate teams in Austin, Dallas, and Houston use waterways like Lady Bird Lake for year-round training and regattas.

facts.fact: Lacrosse has expanded rapidly in Texas high schools and clubs over the last two decades.
facts.background: Originally concentrated in the Northeast, the sport now has strong participation and competitive high school programs in the major Texas metro areas.

facts.fact: Texas hosts a number of major fishing tournaments on lakes like Sam Rayburn, Toledo Bend, and Lake Fork.
facts.background: These events draw professional and amateur anglers seeking trophy bass and substantial prize money.

facts.fact: Hunting is a major outdoor sport in Texas, with seasons for deer, dove, turkey, and other game.
facts.background: Both private ranches and public lands support a large hunting community, contributing significantly to the state’s outdoor recreation economy.

facts.fact: The state’s size and varied terrain make Texas a popular destination for trail running and mountain biking events.
facts.background: Hill Country trails, desert landscapes in West Texas, and wooded courses in East Texas host events from 5Ks to ultramarathons.

facts.fact: Cheerleading and drill team competitions are a highly visible part of Texas high school sports culture.
facts.background: Teams spend months preparing choreographed routines and often compete for state and national titles.

facts.fact: Esports has become a growing competitive activity in Texas schools and universities.
facts.background: High schools and colleges have launched esports teams that compete in games like League of Legends and Rocket League in organized leagues.

facts.fact: Texas is home to multiple large running relay and adventure races, including long-distance team events crossing rural parts of the state.
facts.background: These relay races combine endurance running with road trips and are popular among clubs and corporate teams.

facts.fact: Many Texas cities support thriving pickup basketball, soccer, and flag football scenes in public parks and rec centers.
facts.background: Year-round warm weather allows outdoor courts and fields in Houston, Austin, Dallas, and San Antonio to stay busy nearly every day of the week.

facts.fact: Texas’ strong sports culture is reinforced by extensive local media coverage of high school and college athletics.
facts.background: Local TV stations, newspapers, and dedicated websites regularly lead with high school football, regional college sports, and local pro teams.

facts.fact: The NFHS has highlighted Texas as an example of high participation and community investment in school sports.
facts.background: Reports note that Texas’ combination of large enrollment, strong booster support, and deep traditions makes school sports central to community life.

facts.fact: From football and rodeo to NASCAR and soccer, Texas touches nearly every major sport played in the United States.
facts.background: The state’s population, geography, and culture support a deep sports ecosystem, from youth leagues and high schools to college and professional teams.

facts.fact: The flags of six nations have flown over Texas: Spain, France, Mexico, the Republic of Texas, the Confederate States, and the United States.
facts.background: Texas history is often summarized as the "Six Flags of Texas," reflecting changes in sovereignty over centuries.
facts.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission

facts.fact: Delegates at Washington-on-the-Brazos adopted the Texas Declaration of Independence on March 2, 1836.
facts.background: The convention declared Texas a free and independent republic as fighting continued during the Texas Revolution.
facts.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission

facts.fact: The Republic of Texas existed from 1836 until Texas statehood in 1845.
facts.background: Texas functioned as an independent nation for nearly a decade between independence and annexation.
facts.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission

facts.fact: Texas was admitted to the United States on December 29, 1845.
facts.background: After years of diplomacy and debate, Congress approved Texas statehood late in 1845.
facts.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission

facts.fact: Texas joined the Confederacy in 1861 after a secession convention approved an ordinance of secession.
facts.background: The Civil War period marked another major change in Texas government and allegiance.
facts.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission

facts.fact: Juneteenth commemorates June 19, 1865, when General Order No. 3 was announced in Galveston.
facts.background: The date marks when news of emancipation was formally enforced in Texas.
facts.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission

facts.fact: Texas still operates under the state constitution adopted in 1876.
facts.background: The 1876 constitution remains in force, although it has been amended many times.

facts.fact: The Spindletop gusher near Beaumont in 1901 helped launch Texas' modern oil boom.
facts.background: The discovery transformed Texas industry and accelerated growth in the Gulf Coast region.

facts.fact: The Galveston hurricane of 1900 is widely recognized as the deadliest natural disaster in U.S. history.
facts.background: The storm devastated Galveston and changed coastal planning and weather warning systems.

facts.fact: The current Texas State Capitol was dedicated in 1888.
facts.background: The granite capitol in Austin became a central symbol of Texas state government.
facts.source: Texas State Preservation Board / Texas State Library

facts.fact: Texas shares borders with four U.S. states: New Mexico, Oklahoma, Arkansas, and Louisiana.
facts.background: Its central location links the Southwest, South, and Gulf Coast regions of the United States.
facts.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission / U.S. Census

facts.fact: The U.S. Census lists Texas land area at 261,267.85 square miles (2020).
facts.background: Texas spans deserts, plains, forests, hill country, and a long Gulf Coast.

facts.fact: Texas is the second-largest U.S. state by total area, behind Alaska.
facts.background: Its size contributes to major regional differences in climate, culture, and terrain.

facts.fact: Guadalupe Peak is the highest natural point in Texas at 8,751 feet above sea level.
facts.background: The peak is in Guadalupe Mountains National Park in far West Texas.

facts.fact: The Brazos River runs about 840 miles and is the longest river in Texas.
facts.background: The river flows from the High Plains to the Gulf Coast and has shaped settlement and agriculture.

facts.fact: The Trinity is the longest river whose entire course lies within Texas.
facts.background: The river system passes through North and East Texas and reaches Trinity Bay.

facts.fact: Palo Duro Canyon in the Panhandle is often described as the second-largest canyon in the United States.
facts.background: The canyon's size and layered geology make it one of Texas' signature landforms.

facts.fact: Most of Texas uses Central Time, but parts of far West Texas observe Mountain Time.
facts.background: Traffic and transportation guidance identify El Paso and Hudspeth county areas in the Mountain zone.

facts.fact: The Rio Grande forms a major portion of the Texas-Mexico international boundary.
facts.background: Border geography has long influenced trade, migration, and regional culture.
facts.source: Texas State Library / Handbook of Texas

facts.fact: Texas has a long coastline on the Gulf of Mexico.
facts.background: Coastal bays, estuaries, and ports are central to Texas ecology and commerce.
facts.source: Texas General Land Office / Texas Almanac

facts.fact: 'Don't Mess with Texas' began in 1986 as an anti-litter campaign.
facts.background: The slogan became one of the most recognizable public campaigns associated with the state.

facts.fact: 'Texas, Our Texas' is the official state song.
facts.background: The Legislature adopted it in 1929, and it remains a formal civic symbol.
facts.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission

facts.fact: Chili is the official state dish of Texas.
facts.background: The designation reflects the state's long culinary identity tied to chili cook-offs and regional food traditions.

facts.fact: Tortilla chips and salsa are the official state snack of Texas.
facts.background: The snack was designated by the Legislature in 2003.

facts.fact: The square dance is the official state folk dance of Texas.
facts.background: The designation reflects community dance traditions across generations.

facts.fact: Rodeo is the official state sport of Texas.
facts.background: The Legislature designated rodeo in 1997, recognizing ranching and competition traditions.

facts.fact: The cowboy hat is an official state symbol of Texas.
facts.background: The designation highlights cowboy culture's influence on Texas identity.

facts.fact: The cowboy boot is an official state symbol of Texas.
facts.background: Bootmaking and western wear are enduring parts of Texas style and heritage.

facts.fact: The guitar is the official state musical instrument of Texas.
facts.background: The designation reflects Texas' deep influence on country, blues, Tejano, and rock music traditions.

facts.fact: Sopaipilla and strudel are the official state pastries of Texas.
facts.background: The dual designation reflects the blend of culinary traditions found across Texas communities.

facts.fact: The roots of the UIL date to 1910, and the modern UIL was formed in 1913.
facts.background: The league grew from early debating and athletic associations connected to the University of Texas.

facts.fact: The UIL describes itself as the largest inter-school organization of its kind in the world.
facts.background: It oversees academic, music, and athletic competition for Texas public schools.

facts.fact: Texas Motor Speedway hosted its first NASCAR doubleheader weekend in April 1997.
facts.background: The event marked the start of major NASCAR competition at the Fort Worth-area track.

facts.fact: The Dallas Cowboys were awarded an NFL expansion franchise in 1960.
facts.background: The franchise quickly became one of the league's most recognized teams.

facts.fact: The Houston Texans became the NFL's 32nd franchise during the 2002 season.
facts.background: Their inaugural year restored an NFL team to Houston.

facts.fact: Houston's MLB franchise debuted as the Colt .45s in 1962.
facts.background: The team played its first major league game in Houston on April 10, 1962.

facts.fact: Houston's MLB team changed its name from Colt .45s to Astros for the 1965 season.
facts.background: The new name aligned with the move into the Astrodome and Houston's space-age identity.

facts.fact: As of the 2025 season, Texas has made more Men's College World Series appearances than any other program.
facts.background: NCAA records through 2025 list Texas at the top for CWS trips.

facts.fact: As of the 2025 season, the Texas Longhorns have won six Men's College World Series national championships.
facts.background: Texas is one of college baseball's most decorated programs.

facts.fact: As of the 2025 season, Texas programs have won seven Men's College World Series titles in total.
facts.background: NCAA lists six titles for Texas and one for Rice through the 2025 season.

facts.fact: The Texas Constitution vests legislative power in a Senate and House of Representatives.
