facts.background: Together, the two chambers form the Texas Legislature.

facts.fact: The Texas House of Representatives has 150 members.
facts.background: House membership size is set in the state constitution.

facts.fact: The Texas Senate has 31 members.
facts.background: Senators represent districts of roughly equal population across the state.

facts.fact: Regular Texas legislative sessions occur in odd-numbered years and may last up to 140 days.
facts.background: Session timing and duration are core features of Texas lawmaking.

facts.fact: Texas' executive department includes the governor, lieutenant governor, secretary of state, comptroller, land commissioner, and attorney general.
facts.background: Texas uses a plural executive model that distributes power across multiple statewide offices.

facts.fact: Texas governors serve four-year terms.
facts.background: The governor's term length is defined in the state constitution.

facts.fact: Texas judicial power is vested in multiple court levels, including a Supreme Court and a Court of Criminal Appeals.
facts.background: Article V lays out a multi-tier court structure for civil and criminal matters.

facts.fact: Texas has two courts of last resort: the Supreme Court for civil matters and the Court of Criminal Appeals for criminal cases.
facts.background: This split high-court structure is unusual among U.S. states.

facts.fact: Texas now has 15 courts of appeals, with the first 14 serving regional districts and the 15th holding specialized statewide jurisdiction.
facts.background: Intermediate appellate jurisdiction expanded with creation of the Fifteenth Court of Appeals.

facts.fact: Texas does not levy a state personal income tax.
facts.background: State revenue relies on other taxes and fees, including sales taxes.

facts.fact: Texas' state sales and use tax rate is 6.25%.
facts.background: Local jurisdictions may add sales taxes within state limits.

facts.fact: Local sales and use taxes in Texas cannot exceed 2%, making the maximum combined sales tax rate 8.25%.
facts.background: State and local rate caps are defined in tax law and comptroller guidance.

facts.fact: Texas franchise tax is a privilege tax on taxable entities formed in Texas or doing business in Texas.
facts.background: Businesses report and pay franchise tax annually under state rules.

facts.fact: Texas has no state property tax; property taxes are imposed by local taxing units.
facts.background: School districts, cities, counties, and special districts set local property tax rates.

facts.fact: The U.S. Energy Information Administration identifies Texas as the top crude-oil-producing state.
facts.background: Texas energy production remains central to both the state and national economy.

facts.fact: In 2024, Texas led the nation in wind-powered electricity generation.
facts.background: EIA reports Texas produced nearly three-tenths of U.S. wind-sourced electricity that year.

facts.fact: Texas generates more total electricity than any other U.S. state.
facts.background: Its large population, industrial base, and generation fleet drive nation-leading output.

facts.fact: Texas' 6.25% state sales tax rate has been in effect since July 1, 1990.
facts.background: Historical tax-rate tables show the current state rate has remained unchanged since 1990.

facts.fact: Sam Houston became the first regularly elected president of the Republic of Texas in 1836.
facts.background: Houston was one of the central political and military figures of early Texas history.

facts.fact: Sam Houston served as both the first and third president of the Republic of Texas.
facts.background: Constitutional term limits prevented immediate reelection, so he returned for a later term.

facts.fact: Lyndon B. Johnson was born on August 27, 1908, near Stonewall in the Texas Hill Country.
facts.background: The future 36th U.S. president grew up in Central Texas.

facts.fact: Dwight D. Eisenhower was born in Denison, Texas, on October 14, 1890.
facts.background: He later became the 34th president of the United States.

facts.fact: Barbara Jordan was elected to the Texas Senate in 1966 as the first Black woman to serve in that body.
facts.background: Her election marked a major milestone in Texas and U.S. political history.

facts.fact: In 1972, Barbara Jordan was elected to the U.S. House as the first woman elected in her own right to represent Texas.
facts.background: She became a nationally influential voice on constitutional issues.

facts.fact: Barbara Jordan became the first Black woman from a Southern state elected to the U.S. House of Representatives.
facts.background: Her congressional service broke long-standing regional barriers in national politics.

facts.fact: Aviator Bessie Coleman was born in Atlanta, Texas.
facts.background: Coleman's early life in Texas preceded her pioneering aviation career.
facts.source: Texas Historical Commission / National Park Service

facts.fact: In 1921, Bessie Coleman became the first Black American and first American woman to earn an international pilot's license.
facts.background: She trained in France after U.S. schools refused to train her.

facts.fact: Texas designated the bluebonnet as the official state flower in 1901.
facts.background: Bluebonnets bloom widely in spring and are one of the state's most recognized natural symbols.
facts.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission

facts.fact: The mockingbird is the official state bird of Texas.
facts.background: Its adaptability and song made it a long-standing symbol in Texas wildlife identity.
facts.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission

facts.fact: The pecan tree is the official state tree of Texas.
facts.background: Pecans are native to Texas river bottoms and important to state agriculture and cuisine.
facts.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission

facts.fact: The Guadalupe bass is the official state fish of Texas.
facts.background: It is one of Texas' signature freshwater sport fish.

facts.fact: The Guadalupe bass is found only in Texas in the wild.
facts.background: The species is endemic to Texas and has been a focus of conservation work in Hill Country rivers.

facts.fact: The nine-banded armadillo is the official state small mammal of Texas.
facts.background: The armadillo was designated as an official state mammal symbol in 1995.

facts.fact: The Texas longhorn is the official state large mammal of Texas.
facts.background: The longhorn remains a defining animal in Texas ranching history.

facts.fact: The Mexican free-tailed bat is the official state flying mammal of Texas.
facts.background: Large bat colonies in Texas caves are among the most visible examples of this species.

facts.fact: The monarch butterfly is the official state insect of Texas.
facts.background: Monarch migration routes and habitat in Texas make it a fitting symbol designation.

facts.fact: The Houston Ship Channel's opening was ceremonially observed on November 10, 1914.
facts.background: The channel established Houston as a major deep-water port and trade hub.

facts.fact: Dallas Fort Worth International Airport began operations on January 13, 1974.
facts.background: DFW grew into one of the world's busiest and most connected airports.

facts.fact: DFW Airport covers more than 26.9 square miles (17,183 acres).
facts.background: Its land area makes it one of the largest airports by footprint.

facts.fact: DFW Airport currently operates seven runways.
facts.background: Runway capacity supports major domestic and international passenger and cargo traffic.

facts.fact: TxDOT reports jurisdiction over 81,035 centerline miles of roadway (2024).
facts.background: The state-maintained network includes interstates, U.S. routes, and other on-system roads.

facts.fact: TxDOT's statewide monitoring program conducts more than 75,000 short-term traffic counts each year.
facts.background: Traffic counts support planning, safety analysis, and federal reporting.

facts.fact: Texas law allows speed limits up to 85 mph on highway segments designed and studied for that speed.
facts.background: Higher posted limits require engineering and safety review.

facts.fact: The Texas Highway Trunk System is limited to 11,500 miles by commission minute order.
facts.background: The network is intended to connect major cities, ports, and entry points.

facts.fact: TxDOT's departmental map visualizes on-system routes including interstate, U.S., state, and farm-to-market roads.
facts.background: The map is updated and published to show roads maintained by TxDOT.

facts.fact: NASA's Johnson Space Center was established in 1961 as the Manned Spacecraft Center.
facts.background: The center became NASA's primary home for human spaceflight operations.

facts.fact: NASA announced Houston as the site for its new human-spaceflight center on September 19, 1961.
facts.background: Site selection anchored Houston's long-term role in U.S. space exploration.

facts.fact: The Manned Spacecraft Center officially opened for business in Houston on November 1, 1961.
facts.background: Work began in temporary offices while the permanent Clear Lake campus was built.

facts.fact: The center was renamed the Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center in 1973.
facts.background: The name honors the Texas-born president associated with the U.S. space program era.

facts.fact: Johnson Space Center has long served as mission control and astronaut training headquarters for U.S. human spaceflight.
facts.background: Programs from Gemini and Apollo through ISS-era missions have relied on Houston operations.

facts.fact: UT Austin's Texas Advanced Computing Center launched the Frontera supercomputer in 2019.
facts.background: Frontera expanded U.S. academic capacity for advanced computing research.
facts.source: University of Texas at Austin / TACC

facts.fact: At launch, Frontera was described as the fastest supercomputer at any university and ranked No. 5 worldwide.
facts.background: The system achieved top-tier performance on the TOP500 benchmark list.
facts.source: University of Texas at Austin / TACC

facts.fact: Rice University scientists discovered carbon-60 (buckminsterfullerene) in 1985, work that led to the 1996 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.
facts.background: The discovery became a landmark in nanoscience and materials chemistry.

facts.fact: Article VII of the Texas Constitution is dedicated to education and public free schools.
facts.background: The article establishes core constitutional commitments for public education in Texas.

facts.fact: The Texas Permanent School Fund was created in 1854 with a $2 million legislative appropriation.
facts.background: The fund was established for the benefit of Texas public schools.

facts.fact: Texas A&M opened on October 4, 1876, as the state's first public institution of higher education.
facts.background: The school began as the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas.

facts.fact: Texas A&M began instruction with 40 students and six faculty members.
facts.background: The university grew from a small founding class into one of the largest campuses in the nation.

facts.fact: The University of Texas officially opened in 1883.
facts.background: UT's launch fulfilled decades of efforts to create a major public university for Texas.

facts.fact: When UT opened, classes started with eight professors and 221 students.
facts.background: Early classes were held while the first main campus building was being completed.

facts.fact: Texas A&M was created under the land-grant mission established by the Morrill Act of 1862.
facts.background: The act funded colleges focused on agriculture and the mechanical arts.

facts.fact: In 1963, the Texas Legislature changed the school's name from the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas to Texas A&M University.
facts.background: The rename reflected the institution's broader academic scope.
